**Kerala**[[a]](#cite_note-15) is an [Indian](/wiki/India "India") state on the [Malabar Coast](/wiki/Malabar_Coast "Malabar Coast"). It was formed on 1 November 1956 under the [States Reorganisation Act](/wiki/States_Reorganisation_Act "States Reorganisation Act"), which unified the country's [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam")-speaking regions into a single state. Covering 38,863 km2 (15,005 sq mi), it is bordered by [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka") to the north and northeast, [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu") to the east and south, and the [Laccadive Sea](/wiki/Laccadive_Sea "Laccadive Sea") to the west. With 33 million inhabitants according to the [2011 census](/wiki/2011_Census_of_India "2011 Census of India"), Kerala is the [13th-most populous state in India](/wiki/List_of_states_of_India_by_population "List of states of India by population"). It is divided into [14 districts](/wiki/List_of_districts_of_Kerala "List of districts of Kerala"), with [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram "Thiruvananthapuram") as the capital. Malayalam is the most widely spoken language and, along with English, serves as an official language of the state.

Kerala has been a prominent exporter of spices since 3000 [BCE](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era"). The [Chera dynasty](/wiki/Chera_dynasty "Chera dynasty"), the first major kingdom in the region, rose to prominence through maritime commerce but often faced invasions from the neighbouring [Chola](/wiki/Chola "Chola") and [Pandya](/wiki/Pandya "Pandya") dynasties. In the 15th century, the [spice trade](/wiki/Spice_trade "Spice trade") attracted Portuguese traders to Kerala, initiating [European colonisation](/wiki/Colonial_India "Colonial India") in India.

After [Indian independence](/wiki/Indian_independence_movement "Indian independence movement") in 1947, [Travancore](/wiki/Travancore "Travancore") and [Cochin](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Cochin "Kingdom of Cochin") acceded to the newly formed republic and were merged in 1949 to form the state of [Travancore-Cochin](/wiki/Travancore-Cochin "Travancore-Cochin"). In 1956, the modern state of Kerala was formed by merging the [Malabar district](/wiki/Malabar_district "Malabar district"), Travancore-Cochin (excluding four southern [taluks](/wiki/Taluk "Taluk")), and the [Kasargod](/wiki/Kasargod "Kasargod") taluk of [South Kanara](/wiki/South_Kanara "South Kanara").

Kerala has the highest [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index"), at 0.784 in 2018; the highest literacy rate, 96.2% in 2018; the highest life expectancy, at 77.3 years; and the highest [sex ratio](/wiki/Sex_ratio "Sex ratio"), with 1,084 women per 1,000 men and the lowest positive population growth rate in India (3.44%). It is the [least impoverished](/wiki/List_of_Indian_states_and_union_territories_by_poverty_rate "List of Indian states and union territories by poverty rate") and the [second-most urbanised](/wiki/Urbanisation_in_India "Urbanisation in India") state in the country. The state has witnessed significant emigration, particularly to the [Arab states of the Persian Gulf](/wiki/Arab_states_of_the_Persian_Gulf "Arab states of the Persian Gulf") during the [Gulf Boom](/wiki/Kerala_Gulf_diaspora#The_Gulf_Boom "Kerala Gulf diaspora") of the 1970s and early 1980s, and its economy relies heavily on remittances from a large [Malayali](/wiki/Malayali "Malayali") expatriate population. Hinduism is practised by more than [54% of the population](/wiki/Religion_in_Kerala "Religion in Kerala"), followed by Islam and Christianity. The culture is a synthesis of [Aryan](/wiki/Aryan "Aryan") and [Dravidian](/wiki/Dravidian_peoples "Dravidian peoples") traditions, shaped over millennia by influences from across India and abroad.

The production of [black pepper](/wiki/Black_pepper "Black pepper") and [natural rubber](/wiki/Natural_rubber "Natural rubber") contributes significantly to the national output. In the agricultural sector, coconut, tea, coffee, cashew, and spices are important crops. The state has a coastline of 595 kilometres (370 mi), and 1.1 million people depend on the fishing industry, which accounts for around 3% of the state's income. The economy is largely service sector oriented, while the primary sector contributes a comparatively smaller share. Kerala has the [highest media exposure in India](/wiki/Indian_states_ranking_by_media_exposure "Indian states ranking by media exposure"), with newspapers published in nine languages, primarily Malayalam and English.

Named as one of the ten paradises of the world by [National Geographic Traveler](/wiki/National_Geographic_Traveler "National Geographic Traveler"), Kerala is one of the prominent [tourist destinations](/wiki/Tourism_in_Kerala "Tourism in Kerala") of India, with [coconut-lined sandy beaches](/wiki/Beaches_in_Kerala "Beaches in Kerala"), [backwaters](/wiki/Kerala_Backwaters "Kerala Backwaters"), [hill stations](/wiki/Hill_station "Hill station"), [Ayurvedic tourism](/wiki/Ayurveda "Ayurveda") and [tropical](/wiki/Tropical "Tropical") greenery as its major attractions. The state has been repeatedly recognised in international travel rankings for its natural landscape and cultural heritage.

## Etymology

The word *Kerala* is first recorded as *Keralaputo* ('son of [Chera](/wiki/Chera_dynasty "Chera dynasty") [s]') in a 3rd-century-BCE rock inscription left by the [Maurya emperor](/wiki/Maurya_Empire "Maurya Empire") [Ashoka](/wiki/Ashoka "Ashoka") (274–237 BCE), one of [his edicts](/wiki/Edicts_of_Ashoka "Edicts of Ashoka") pertaining to welfare.[[15]](#cite_note-16) At that time, one of three states in the region was called [*Cheralam*](/wiki/Chera_Dynasty "Chera Dynasty") in Classical Tamil: *Chera* and *Kera* are variants of the same word.[[16]](#cite_note-Sainz1972-17) The word *Cheral* refers to the oldest known dynasty of Kerala kings and is derived from the [Old Tamil](/wiki/Old_Tamil "Old Tamil") word for 'lake'.[[17]](#cite_note-Marr1985-18) *Keralam* may stem from the Classical [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") *cherive-alam* 'declivity of a hill or a mountain slope'[[18]](#cite_note-AlexDeborah2014-19) or *chera alam* 'land of the Cheras'. One [folk etymology](/wiki/Folk_etymology "Folk etymology") derives *Kerala* from the [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") word *kera* 'coconut tree' and *alam* 'land'; thus, 'land of coconuts',[[19]](#cite_note-Sadasivan2000-20) which is a nickname for the state used by locals due to the abundance of coconut trees.[[20]](#cite_note-PreedyWatson2011-21) Kerala was alternatively called *[Malabar](/wiki/Malabar_Coast "Malabar Coast")* in the [foreign trade circles](/wiki/Indian_Ocean_trade "Indian Ocean trade"). From the time of [Cosmas Indicopleustes](/wiki/Cosmas_Indicopleustes "Cosmas Indicopleustes") (6th century CE) itself, the [Arab](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs") sailors used to call Kerala *Male*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Keralam

There have been long-standing demands for changing the name to Keralam, the [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") name of the state. Officially, the state's name in [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") is ‘Keralam’ *(കേരളം)*, while its English name is ‘Kerala’.[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23) The [Kerala Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Kerala_Legislative_Assembly "Kerala Legislative Assembly") passed a unanimous resolution in June 2024 to change the state's name from "Kerala" to "Keralam," following an earlier unanimous resolution in August 2023. [Chief Minister](/wiki/Chief_Minister_of_Kerala "Chief Minister of Kerala") [Pinarayi Vijayan](/wiki/Pinarayi_Vijayan "Pinarayi Vijayan") had moved both resolutions, which sought to amend the state's name under Article 3 of the Constitution to reflect its [Malayali](/wiki/Malayali "Malayali") identity.[[23]](#cite_note-auto1-24)

The [Union Cabinet](/wiki/Third_Modi_ministry "Third Modi ministry") approved this proposal on February 24, 2026. Following Cabinet approval, the [President of India](/wiki/President_of_India "President of India") will refer the Kerala (Alteration of Name) Bill, 2026 to the [State Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Kerala_Legislative_Assembly "Kerala Legislative Assembly") for expressing its views under the proviso to Article 3 of the Constitution of India.[[24]](#cite_note-auto-25)

After the [Kerala Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Kerala_Legislative_Assembly "Kerala Legislative Assembly")'s approval, this bill will be introduced in the [Parliament of India](/wiki/Parliament_of_India "Parliament of India") and if passed, the state's name will officially become Keralam.

## History

Main article: [History of Kerala](/wiki/History_of_Kerala "History of Kerala")

### Legend

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Parshuramsaraswats.jpg/250px-Parshuramsaraswats.jpg)](/wiki/File:Parshuramsaraswats.jpg)

A painting depicting [Parashurama](/wiki/Parashurama "Parashurama"), surrounded by settlers, commanding [Varuna](/wiki/Varuna "Varuna") (the [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") god of water) to part the seas

In stories of the [Dashavatara](/wiki/Dashavatara "Dashavatara") from [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") mythology, the lands of Kerala were recovered from the sea by the axe-wielding warrior sage [Parashurama](/wiki/Parashurama "Parashurama"), the sixth [avatar](/wiki/Avatar "Avatar") (incarnation) of [Vishnu](/wiki/Vishnu "Vishnu"). As a result, Kerala is traditionally referred to as *Parashurama Kshetram* ("The Land of Parashurama").[[25]](#cite_note-26) According to legend, Parashurama threw his axe across the sea, and the water receded to the point where it landed. This land that emerged extended from [Gokarna](/wiki/Gokarna,_Karnataka "Gokarna, Karnataka") to [Kanyakumari](/wiki/Kanyakumari "Kanyakumari").[[26]](#cite_note-27) The land that emerged was saline and uninhabitable, so Parashurama invoked the snake king [Vasuki](/wiki/Vasuki "Vasuki"), who spat holy poison to purify the soil, transforming it into fertile land. Out of respect, Vasuki and all snakes were appointed as guardians of the land. The legend was expanded and codified in the 17th or 18th century text *[Keralolpathi](/wiki/Keralolpathi "Keralolpathi")*. It links the origin of early Kerala institutions—such as land tenure and administration—to Parashurama's story.[[27]](#cite_note-28) In medieval times, the [Chera](/wiki/Chera "Chera") king [Chenkuttuvan](/wiki/Chenkuttuvan "Chenkuttuvan") may have emulated the Parashurama tradition by throwing his spear into the sea to symbolise his lordship over it.[[28]](#cite_note-29)

A prominent [Puranic](/wiki/Puranic "Puranic") figure associated with Kerala is [Mahabali](/wiki/Mahabali "Mahabali"), an [asura](/wiki/Asura "Asura") and archetypal just king who is said to have ruled the earth from Kerala. He defeated the [devas](/wiki/Deva_(Hinduism) "Deva (Hinduism)") in battle, driving them into exile. In response, the devas appealed to Vishnu, who assumed his fifth avatar as [Vamana](/wiki/Vamana "Vamana") and, to restore order, pushed Mahabali down to [Patala](/wiki/Patala "Patala") (the netherworld). According to popular belief, Mahabali returns to Kerala once a year, which is commemorated as the [Onam](/wiki/Onam "Onam") festival.[[29]](#cite_note-Rinehart2004-30) The *[Matsya Purana](/wiki/Matsya_Purana "Matsya Purana")*, one of the oldest among the 18 [Puranas](/wiki/Puranas "Puranas"),[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32) situates the story of [Matsya](/wiki/Matsya "Matsya")—the first avatar of Vishnu—and king [Manu](/wiki/Manu_(Hinduism) "Manu (Hinduism)"), the first man and ruler of the region—in the [Malaya Mountains](/wiki/Malaya_Mountains "Malaya Mountains") of Kerala and [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu").[[32]](#cite_note-33)[[33]](#cite_note-34)

### Pre-history

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/MarayoorDolmen.JPG/250px-MarayoorDolmen.JPG)](/wiki/File:MarayoorDolmen.JPG)

A [dolmen](/wiki/Dolmen "Dolmen") erected by Neolithic people in [Marayur](/wiki/Marayur "Marayur")

A substantial portion of present-day Kerala is believed to have been submerged under the sea in ancient times. The discovery of marine fossils near Changanassery supports this hypothesis.[[34]](#cite_note-Menon2007-35) Prehistoric archaeological discoveries in Kerala include [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic")-era [dolmens](/wiki/Dolmen "Dolmen") in the [Marayur](/wiki/Marayur "Marayur") region of the Idukki district, locally known as *muniyara*—from *muni* (hermit or sage) and *ara* (dolmen).[[35]](#cite_note-Unlocking_the_secrets_of_history-36) Rock engravings in the [Edakkal caves](/wiki/Edakkal_caves "Edakkal caves") in [Wayanad](/wiki/Wayanad "Wayanad") date back to the Neolithic period, around 6000 BCE.[[36]](#cite_note-Kapoor2002-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38) Archaeological studies have identified [Mesolithic](/wiki/Mesolithic "Mesolithic"), Neolithic and [Megalithic](/wiki/Megalithic "Megalithic") sites throughout the region.[[38]](#cite_note-AroraSingh116-39) These findings indicate that the development of early Kerala society and culture began in the [Paleolithic Age](/wiki/Paleolithic "Paleolithic") and progressed through the Mesolithic, Neolithic and Megalithic periods.[[39]](#cite_note-google-40) Foreign cultural interactions also played a role in shaping this development;[[40]](#cite_note-AroraSingh1999-41) some historians suggest possible connections with the [Indus Valley Civilisation](/wiki/Indus_Valley_Civilisation "Indus Valley Civilisation") during the late [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") and early [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age").[[41]](#cite_note-42)

### Ancient history

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Silk_route.jpg/250px-Silk_route.jpg)](/wiki/File:Silk_route.jpg)

[Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road") map. The spice trade was along the water routes (blue).

Kerala has been a major spice exporter since at least 3000 BCE, according to [Sumerian records](/wiki/Sumer "Sumer"), and it is continues to be referred to as the "Garden of Spices" or as the "Spice Garden of India".[[42]](#cite_note-43)[[43]](#cite_note-44)[[44]](#cite_note-spiceHindu-45) The region's spices attracted ancient [Babylonians](/wiki/Babylonians "Babylonians"), [Assyrians](/wiki/Assyrians "Assyrians"), and [Egyptians](/wiki/Egyptians "Egyptians") to the Malabar Coast during the 3rd and 2nd millennia BCE. [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs "Arabs") and [Phoenicians](/wiki/Phoenicians "Phoenicians") also established trade links with Kerala during this period.[[45]](#cite_note-Menon57-46) The land of Keralaputra was one of four independent kingdoms in southern India during the time of Emperor Ashoka, alongside the [Chola](/wiki/Chola_Empire "Chola Empire"), [Pandya](/wiki/Pandya_Empire "Pandya Empire"), and [Satiyaputra](/wiki/Satiyaputras "Satiyaputras") kingdoms.[[46]](#cite_note-SmithJackson2008-47) Scholars generally identify Keralaputra as another name for the [Cheras](/wiki/Chera_dynasty "Chera dynasty"), the earliest major dynasty based in Kerala.[[47]](#cite_note-The_Cambridge_Shorter_History_of_India-48)[[48]](#cite_note-Dwivedi1994-49) These southern territories once shared a common language and cultural framework, within a region historically known as [Tamilakam](/wiki/Tamilakam "Tamilakam").[[49]](#cite_note-50) While the Cheras governed most of what is now Kerala, the southern tip of the region fell under the control of the [Pandyas](/wiki/Pandyas "Pandyas"),[[50]](#cite_note-Singh2008-51) whose trading port is sometimes identified in ancient Western sources as [Nelcynda](/wiki/Nelcynda "Nelcynda") (or Neacyndi).[[51]](#cite_note-Books.google.co.in-52) Later, control of the region alternated among the Pandyas, Cheras, and Cholas. The [Ays](/wiki/Ay_kingdom "Ay kingdom") and [Mushikas](/wiki/Ezhimala_kingdom "Ezhimala kingdom") were two other dynasties of ancient Kerala, located to the south and north of the Chera territory, respectively.[[52]](#cite_note-Shashi1996-53)[[53]](#cite_note-Ramunny1993-54)

By the last centuries BCE, the coast had become an important hub for Greek and Roman trade, particularly in [black pepper](/wiki/Black_pepper "Black pepper"). The Cheras maintained commercial links with [Ancient China](/wiki/Ancient_China "Ancient China"), [West Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), and the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire"). In foreign trade records, the region was referred to as Male or [Malabar](/wiki/Malabar_Coast "Malabar Coast").[[54]](#cite_note-55) Principal ports of the time included [Muziris](/wiki/Muziris "Muziris"), Berkarai, and Nelcynda.[[55]](#cite_note-Kusuman-56) The value of Rome's annual trade with Kerala has been estimated at around 50 million [sesterces](/wiki/Sesterces "Sesterces").[[56]](#cite_note-57) Contemporary [Sangam literature](/wiki/Sangam_literature "Sangam literature") describes Roman ships arriving at Muziris, laden with gold in exchange for pepper. One of the earliest Western traders to navigate the monsoon winds to reach Kerala was [Eudoxus of Cyzicus](/wiki/Eudoxus_of_Cyzicus "Eudoxus of Cyzicus"), who made the voyage around 118 or 166 BCE under the patronage of [Ptolemy VIII](/wiki/Ptolemy_VIII_Physcon "Ptolemy VIII Physcon"), king of the [Hellenistic](/wiki/Hellenistic_civilisation "Hellenistic civilisation") [Ptolemaic dynasty](/wiki/Ptolemaic_dynasty "Ptolemaic dynasty") in Egypt. Roman establishments in the region's port cities—including a temple of [Augustus](/wiki/Augustus "Augustus") and barracks for garrisoned Roman soldiers—are recorded in the *[Tabula Peutingeriana](/wiki/Tabula_Peutingeriana "Tabula Peutingeriana")*, the only surviving map of the Roman *[cursus publicus](/wiki/Cursus_publicus "Cursus publicus")*.[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-Iyengar_2001-59)

Merchants from West Asia and Southern Europe established coastal posts and settlements in Kerala.[[59]](#cite_note-google2-60) The Jewish connection with Kerala is believed to date back to 573 BCE.[[60]](#cite_note-Orpa_Slapak-61)[[61]](#cite_note-Hillel1832-62)[[62]](#cite_note-63) Arab traders had links with Kerala from at least the 4th century BCE; [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") (484–413 BCE) noted that goods brought by Arabs from Kerala were sold to Jews in Eden.[[55]](#cite_note-Kusuman-56) These Arab traders intermarried with local communities, leading to the formation of the Muslim [Mappila](/wiki/Malabar_Muslims "Malabar Muslims") community.[[63]](#cite_note-Dialogue-64) In the 4th century CE, some Christians migrated from [Persia](/wiki/Persia "Persia") and joined the early [Syrian Christian](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians") community, which traces its origins to the evangelistic activities of [Thomas the Apostle](/wiki/Thomas_the_Apostle "Thomas the Apostle") in the 1st century CE.[[64]](#cite_note-Erwin_Fahlbusch-65)[[65]](#cite_note-66) The term Mappila—originally an honorific applied to esteemed foreign visitors—later became associated with the descendants of Jewish, Syrian Christian, and Muslim immigrants, leading to the terms [Juda Mappilas](/wiki/Juda_Mappila "Juda Mappila"), [Nasrani Mappilas](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians"), and [Muslim Mappilas](/wiki/Malabar_Muslims "Malabar Muslims"), respectively.[[66]](#cite_note-BMalieckal-67)[[67]](#cite_note-68) According to the traditions of these communities, some of the earliest religious establishments in India were built in Kerala.[[63]](#cite_note-Dialogue-64) These include the [Saint Thomas Christian churches](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christian_churches "Saint Thomas Christian churches"),[[68]](#cite_note-69) the [Cheraman Juma Masjid](/wiki/Cheraman_Juma_Masjid "Cheraman Juma Masjid") (established in 629 CE), India's first mosque,[[69]](#cite_note-70) and the [Paradesi Synagogue](/wiki/Paradesi_Synagogue "Paradesi Synagogue") (built in 1568 CE), the oldest active synagogue in the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations").[[70]](#cite_note-71)

### Medieval period

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Quilon_Syrian_copper_plates_%289th_century_AD%29.jpg/250px-Quilon_Syrian_copper_plates_%289th_century_AD%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Quilon_Syrian_copper_plates_(9th_century_AD).jpg)

[Tharisapalli plates](/wiki/Tharisapalli_plates "Tharisapalli plates") granted to [Saint Thomas Christians](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians") testify that merchant guilds and trade corporations played a very significant role in the economy and social life during the Kulasekhara period.

A [second Chera kingdom](/wiki/Later_Chera_Dynasty "Later Chera Dynasty") (c. 800–1102), also known as the Kulasekhara dynasty of [Mahodayapuram](/wiki/Mahodayapuram "Mahodayapuram"), was founded by [Kulasekhara Varman](/wiki/Kulashekhara_Alwar "Kulashekhara Alwar") and governed over a territory comprising most of present-day Kerala and parts of modern Tamil Nadu. During the early phase of the Kulasekara period, the southern region—from [Nagerkovil](/wiki/Nagerkovil "Nagerkovil") to [Thiruvalla](/wiki/Thiruvalla "Thiruvalla")—was controlled by the [Ay dynasty](/wiki/Ays_(Kerala) "Ays (Kerala)"). By the 10th century, however, the Ays had lost their power, and the region was incorporated into the Kulashekara realm.[[71]](#cite_note-Balachand-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73) Under Kulashekhara rule, Kerala experienced a flourishing period marked by developments in art, literature, trade, and the [Bhakti movement](/wiki/Bhakti_movement "Bhakti movement") within Hinduism.[[73]](#cite_note-Sreedhar123-74) It was during this time that a distinct Keralite identity began to emerge, separate from Tamil culture, particularly through linguistic differentiation.[[74]](#cite_note-AsherKumari1997-75) For administrative purposes, the kingdom was divided into provinces governed by local chieftains called [Naduvazhis](/wiki/Naduvazhi "Naduvazhi"). Each province was further subdivided into *desams*, which were overseen by Desavazhis.[[73]](#cite_note-Sreedhar123-74)

A series of Chera–Chola conflicts in the 11th century disrupted foreign trade through Kerala's ports. During this period, [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") and [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"), which had previously coexisted with Hinduism, declined and eventually disappeared from the region.[[75]](#cite_note-76) Social structures became increasingly rigid, and [caste divisions](/wiki/Caste_system_in_Kerala "Caste system in Kerala") deepened.[[76]](#cite_note-77) The Kulashekhara dynasty ultimately fell in 1102 CE following a combined assault by the [Later Pandyas](/wiki/Later_Pandyas "Later Pandyas") and [Later Cholas](/wiki/Later_Cholas "Later Cholas").[[71]](#cite_note-Balachand-72) In the 14th century, Ravi Varma Kulashekhara (r. 1299–1314) of the southern [Venad kingdom](/wiki/Venad "Venad") briefly established a short-lived supremacy over much of southern India. Following his death, and in the absence of a strong central authority, the region fragmented into thirty small, frequently warring principalities. Among the most powerful were the kingdom of [Samuthiri](/wiki/Samuthiri "Samuthiri") (Zamorin) in the north, Venad in the south and Kochi in the central region. In the 18th century, King [Anizham Thirunal Marthanda Varma](/wiki/Anizham_Thirunal_Marthanda_Varma "Anizham Thirunal Marthanda Varma") of Travancore launched a series of military campaigns and annexed territories up to northern Kerala, establishing Travancore as the dominant power in the region. The Kochi ruler sued for peace, and the Malabar region eventually came under direct [British rule](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") until [Indian independence](/wiki/Independence_of_India "Independence of India") in 1947.[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79)

### Colonial rule

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Caminho_maritimo_para_a_India.png/250px-Caminho_maritimo_para_a_India.png)](/wiki/File:Caminho_maritimo_para_a_India.png)

The route taken by [Vasco da Gama](/wiki/Vasco_da_Gama "Vasco da Gama") to reach Kerala (indicated in black)

During the [High](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages") and [Late Middle Ages](/wiki/Late_Middle_Ages "Late Middle Ages"), Arab traders held a maritime monopoly over the spice trade in the [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean"). This dominance was challenged during the European [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery") when the spice trade—particularly in black pepper—became a major focus of European commercial activity.[[79]](#cite_note-80) By the 15th century, the Portuguese had begun asserting control over eastern maritime routes, culminating in [Vasco Da Gama](/wiki/Vasco_Da_Gama "Vasco Da Gama")'s arrival at [Kappad](/wiki/Kappad "Kappad"), near [Kozhikode](/wiki/Kozhikode "Kozhikode") (Calicut), in 1498.[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-Curtin1984-82) The [Zamorin](/wiki/Zamorin "Zamorin") of Kozhikode granted the Portuguese permission to trade with his subjects, leading to the establishment of a prosperous Portuguese [factory](/wiki/Factory_(trading_post) "Factory (trading post)") and fort.[[82]](#cite_note-Mehta2005-83)

However, tensions arose when the Portuguese attacked Arab traders operating under the Zamorin's protection, causing a breakdown in relations. Seizing the opportunity presented by rivalry between the Zamorin and the King of Kochi, the Portuguese allied with Kochi. When [Francisco de Almeida](/wiki/Francisco_de_Almeida "Francisco de Almeida") was appointed Viceroy of Portuguese India in 1505, he established his headquarters at [Fort Kochi](/wiki/Fort_Kochi "Fort Kochi") ([Fort Emmanuel](/wiki/Fort_Emmanuel "Fort Emmanuel")), rather than in Kozhikode. Under his administration, the Portuguese consolidated their influence by building several fortifications along the Malabar Coast.[[82]](#cite_note-Mehta2005-83) Despite these advances, the Portuguese faced significant resistance from the Zamorin's naval forces, particularly under the command of the [Kunjali Marakkars](/wiki/Kunjali_Marakkar "Kunjali Marakkar")—admirals of Kozhikode—who launched effective maritime campaigns. This resistance eventually forced the Portuguese to seek a treaty. In 1571, the Zamorin's forces defeated the Portuguese in the [Battle of Chaliyam Fort](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Tanur#Battles_at_Chaliyam_Fort "Kingdom of Tanur"), marking a major setback for Portuguese ambitions in the region.[[83]](#cite_note-84)

The Portuguese were eventually supplanted by the [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company"), which capitalised on ongoing conflicts between the Kozhikode and Kochi to gain control over trade in the region.[[84]](#cite_note-South_Asia_2006-85) However, the Dutch too faced military resistance, most notably from [Marthanda Varma](/wiki/Marthanda_Varma "Marthanda Varma") of the [Travancore royal family](/wiki/Travancore_royal_family "Travancore royal family"). After a decisive Dutch defeat at the [Battle of Colachel](/wiki/Battle_of_Colachel "Battle of Colachel") in 1741,[[85]](#cite_note-google3-86) the Treaty of Mavelikkara was signed in 1753, forcing the Dutch to withdraw from regional political affairs and confining their role to trade.[[86]](#cite_note-Singh2010-87)[[87]](#cite_note-Iyer1995-88)[[88]](#cite_note-Lannoy1997-89) Marthanda Varma continued his military campaigns, establishing Travancore as the preeminent power in Kerala.[[89]](#cite_note-90)

In 1766, [Hyder Ali](/wiki/Hyder_Ali "Hyder Ali"), ruler of [Mysore](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mysore "Kingdom of Mysore"), [invaded northern Kerala](/wiki/Mysorean_invasion_of_Kerala#Invasion_by_Hyder_Ali "Mysorean invasion of Kerala"),[[90]](#cite_note-Rai-91) and his son and successor, [Tipu Sultan](/wiki/Tipu_Sultan "Tipu Sultan"), [launched military campaigns](/wiki/Mysorean_invasion_of_Kerala#Invasions_by_Tipu_Sultan "Mysorean invasion of Kerala") against the expanding [British East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company"). These confrontations contributed to two of the four [Anglo-Mysore Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Mysore_Wars "Anglo-Mysore Wars").[[91]](#cite_note-MuseumDallapiccola2010-92)[[92]](#cite_note-ThorpeEdgar-93) By the early 1790s, Tipu Sultan was forced to cede the [Malabar district](/wiki/Malabar_district "Malabar district") and [South Kanara](/wiki/South_Kanara "South Kanara") to the British, and these regions were annexed into the [Madras Presidency](/wiki/Madras_Presidency "Madras Presidency") of [British India](/wiki/British_India "British India") in 1792.[[93]](#cite_note-The_Edinburgh_Gazetteer:_Or,_Geographical_Dictionary:_Containing_a_Description_of_the_Various_Countries,_Kingdoms,_States,_Cities,_Towns,_Mountains,_&c._of_the_World;_an_Account_of_the_Government,_Customs,_and_Religion_of_the_Inhabitants;_the_Boundaries_and_Natural_Productions_of_Each_Country,_&c._&c._Forming_a_Complete_Body_of_Geography,_Physical,_Political,_Statistical,_and_Commercial_with_Addenda,_Containing_the_Present_State_of_the_New_Governments_in_South_America...-94)[[94]](#cite_note-Kumar1965-95)[[95]](#cite_note-Ittaman2003-96) The British East India Company also secured tributary alliances with Kochi in 1791 and Travancore in 1795.[[96]](#cite_note-97) By the end of 18th century, the entirety of Kerala was either directly administered by the British or under their [suzerainty](/wiki/Suzerainty "Suzerainty").[[97]](#cite_note-RajTharakan1981-98)

In the 20th century, Kerala was the site of several major uprisings during the Indian independence movement. Among the most notable was the 1921 [Malabar Rebellion](/wiki/Malabar_Rebellion "Malabar Rebellion"), in which Mappila Muslims of the Malabar region rioted against Hindu [zamindars](/wiki/Zamindars "Zamindars") (landlords) and British colonial authorities.[[98]](#cite_note-Qureshi1999-99) Social reform moments also gained momentum during this period, particularly those challenging caste-based discrimination. A major milestone was the [Temple Entry Proclamation](/wiki/Temple_Entry_Proclamation "Temple Entry Proclamation") of 1936 in Travancore, which granted lower-caste Hindus the right to enter temples previously restricted to upper castes.[[99]](#cite_note-Smith1976-100)

### State of India

After the [Partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") in 1947 into the independent dominions of India and Pakistan, the princely states of Travancore and Kochi acceded to the [Union of India](/wiki/Dominion_of_India "Dominion of India"). On 1 July 1949, the two states were merged to form [Travancore-Cochin](/wiki/Travancore-Cochin "Travancore-Cochin").[[100]](#cite_note-Sabharwal2007-101) On 1 November 1956, the [taluk](/wiki/Taluk "Taluk") of [Kasargod](/wiki/Kasaragod_district "Kasaragod district") from the South Kanara district of Madras, the [Malabar district](/wiki/Malabar_district "Malabar district") of [Madras State](/wiki/Madras_State "Madras State") (excluding [Gudalur taluk](/wiki/Gudalur_taluk "Gudalur taluk") of [Nilgiris district](/wiki/Nilgiris_district "Nilgiris district"), [Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep"), [Topslip](/wiki/Topslip "Topslip"), and the [Attappadi](/wiki/Attappadi "Attappadi") forest east of Anakatti), and the state of Travancore-Cochin—excluding four southern taluks ([Kanyakumari district](/wiki/Kanyakumari_district "Kanyakumari district") and Shenkottai taluks), which were transferred to Tamil Nadu—were merged to form the state of Kerala under the [States Reorganisation Act](/wiki/States_Reorganisation_Act "States Reorganisation Act").[[101]](#cite_note-Tmh-102)[[102]](#cite_note-SinghKarafin2009-103)[[103]](#cite_note-Kerala-104) A [Communist](/wiki/Communist "Communist")-led government under [E. M. S. Namboodiripad](/wiki/E._M._S._Namboodiripad "E. M. S. Namboodiripad") was formed following the first elections for the new Kerala Legislative Assembly in 1957.[[102]](#cite_note-SinghKarafin2009-103) It was among the earliest democratically elected Communist governments in the world, following Communist electoral success in the [Republic of San Marino](/wiki/Republic_of_San_Marino "Republic of San Marino") in 1945.[[104]](#cite_note-Hindu-105)[[105]](#cite_note-Desai2006-106)[[106]](#cite_note-ChitkaraŚarmā1997-107)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Kerala](/wiki/Geography_of_Kerala "Geography of Kerala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/AnaimudiPeak_DSC_4834.jpg/250px-AnaimudiPeak_DSC_4834.jpg)](/wiki/File:AnaimudiPeak_DSC_4834.jpg)

[Anamudi](/wiki/Anamudi "Anamudi"), the highest peak in [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/DR0071DSC_9245.jpg/250px-DR0071DSC_9245.jpg)](/wiki/File:DR0071DSC_9245.jpg)

[Tea](/wiki/Tea "Tea") and [Coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee") are produced in the hilly terrains of [Wayanad](/wiki/Wayanad "Wayanad").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Kumarkom.jpg/250px-Kumarkom.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kumarkom.jpg)

[Vembanad](/wiki/Vembanad "Vembanad"), a portion of [Kerala backwaters](/wiki/Kerala_backwaters "Kerala backwaters"), is the longest lake in India.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Light_house_in_Ponnani.jpg/250px-Light_house_in_Ponnani.jpg)](/wiki/File:Light_house_in_Ponnani.jpg)

[Ponnani Lighthouse](/wiki/Ponnani_Lighthouse "Ponnani Lighthouse") beach

The state is wedged between the [Laccadive Sea](/wiki/Laccadive_Sea "Laccadive Sea") and the [Western Ghats](/wiki/Western_Ghats "Western Ghats").[[107]](#cite_note-108) Lying between northern latitudes 8°18' and 12°48' and eastern longitudes 74°52' and 77°22',[[108]](#cite_note-109) Kerala experiences humid [tropical rainforest climate](/wiki/Tropical_rainforest_climate "Tropical rainforest climate") with some cyclones. The state has a coast of 590 km (370 mi)[[109]](#cite_note-110) and the width of the state varies between 11 and 121 kilometres (7 and 75 mi).[[110]](#cite_note-Nair1994-111) Geographically, Kerala can be divided into three climatically distinct regions: the eastern highlands; rugged and cool mountainous terrain, the central mid-lands; rolling hills, and the western lowlands; coastal plains.[[111]](#cite_note-ChattopadhyayFranke2006-112): 110 [Pre-Cambrian](/wiki/Pre-Cambrian "Pre-Cambrian") and [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") geological formations compose the bulk of Kerala's terrain.[[112]](#cite_note-Geological_Survey_Water-supply_Paper-113)[[113]](#cite_note-SharmaKumari2008-114) A catastrophic flood in Kerala in 1341 CE drastically modified its terrain and consequently affected its history; it also created a natural harbour for spice transport.[[114]](#cite_note-LimitedKitchen2010-115)
The eastern region of Kerala consists of high mountains, gorges and deep-cut valleys immediately west of the Western Ghats' [rain shadow](/wiki/Rain_shadow "Rain shadow").[[111]](#cite_note-ChattopadhyayFranke2006-112): 110 41 of Kerala's west-flowing rivers,[[115]](#cite_note-Sadasivan2003-116) and 3 of its east-flowing ones originate in this region.[[116]](#cite_note-Darpan2006-117)[[117]](#cite_note-India2008-118) The Western Ghats form a wall of mountains interrupted only near [Palakkad](/wiki/Palakkad "Palakkad"); hence also known Pal*ghat*, where the [Palakkad Gap](/wiki/Palakkad_Gap "Palakkad Gap") breaks.[[118]](#cite_note-119) The Western Ghats rise on average to 1,500 metres (4,900 [feet](/wiki/Foot_(unit) "Foot (unit)")) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_sea_level "Above sea level"),[[119]](#cite_note-Biju2006-120) while the highest peaks reach around 2,500 metres (8,200 feet).[[120]](#cite_note-Hussain-121) [Anamudi](/wiki/Anamudi "Anamudi") in the [Idukki](/wiki/Idukki "Idukki") district is the highest peak in south India, is at an elevation of 2,695 m (8,842 ft).[[121]](#cite_note-gazetteer-122) The Western Ghats mountain chain is recognised as one of the world's eight "hottest hotspots" of biological diversity and is listed among [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").[[122]](#cite_note-UNESCO-123) The chain's forests are considered to be older than the Himalaya mountains.[[122]](#cite_note-UNESCO-123) The [Athirappilly Falls](/wiki/Athirappilly_Falls "Athirappilly Falls"), which is situated on the background of Western Ghat mountain ranges, is also known as *The [Niagara](/wiki/Niagara_Falls "Niagara Falls") of India*.[[123]](#cite_note-Athirappilly-124) It is located in the [Chalakudy River](/wiki/Chalakudy_River "Chalakudy River") and is the largest waterfall in the state.[[123]](#cite_note-Athirappilly-124) [Wayanad](/wiki/Wayanad "Wayanad") is the sole [Plateau](/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") in Kerala.[[124]](#cite_note-125) The eastern regions in the districts of [Wayanad](/wiki/Wayanad_district "Wayanad district"), [Malappuram](/wiki/Malappuram_district "Malappuram district") ([Chaliyar](/wiki/Chaliyar "Chaliyar") valley at [Nilambur](/wiki/Nilambur "Nilambur")), and [Palakkad](/wiki/Palakkad_district "Palakkad district") ([Attappadi](/wiki/Attappadi "Attappadi") Valley), which together form parts of the [Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Nilgiri_Biosphere_Reserve "Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve") and a continuation of the [Mysore Plateau](/wiki/Mysore_Plateau "Mysore Plateau"), are known for natural [Gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold") fields, along with the adjoining districts of [Karnataka](/wiki/Karnataka "Karnataka").[[125]](#cite_note-126) Minerals including [Ilmenite](/wiki/Ilmenite "Ilmenite"), [Monazite](/wiki/Monazite "Monazite"), [Thorium](/wiki/Thorium "Thorium"), and [Titanium](/wiki/Titanium "Titanium"), are found in the coastal belt of Kerala.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018343-127) Kerala's coastal belt of [Karunagappally](/wiki/Karunagappally "Karunagappally") is known for high background radiation from [thorium](/wiki/Thorium "Thorium")-containing [monazite](/wiki/Monazite "Monazite") sand. In some coastal panchayats, median outdoor radiation levels are more than 4 [mGy/yr](/wiki/Gray_(unit)#Effect_on_the_body "Gray (unit)") and, in certain locations on the coast, it is as high as 70 mGy/yr.[[127]](#cite_note-128)

Kerala's western coastal belt is relatively flat compared to the eastern region,[[111]](#cite_note-ChattopadhyayFranke2006-112): 33 and is criss-crossed by a network of interconnected [brackish](/wiki/Brackish "Brackish") canals, lakes, [estuaries](/wiki/Estuaries "Estuaries"),[[128]](#cite_note-Moss2010-129) and rivers known as the [Kerala Backwaters](/wiki/Kerala_Backwaters "Kerala Backwaters").[[129]](#cite_note-Thorpe2012-130) [Kuttanad](/wiki/Kuttanad "Kuttanad"), also known as *The Rice Bowl of Kerala*, has the [lowest altitude in India](/wiki/List_of_extreme_points_of_India#Altitudes "List of extreme points of India"), and is also one of the few places in world where cultivation takes place below sea level.[[130]](#cite_note-131)[[131]](#cite_note-132) The country's longest lake [Vembanad](/wiki/Vembanad_Lake "Vembanad Lake"), dominates the backwaters; it lies between [Alappuzha](/wiki/Alappuzha "Alappuzha") and [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi") and is about 200 km2 (77 sq mi) in area.[[132]](#cite_note-Husain-133) Around eight percent of India's waterways are found in Kerala.[[133]](#cite_note-IWAI_2005-134) Kerala's [44 rivers](/wiki/List_of_rivers_in_Kerala "List of rivers in Kerala") include the [Periyar](/wiki/Periyar_River "Periyar River"); 244 kilometres (152 mi), [Bharathapuzha](/wiki/Bharathapuzha "Bharathapuzha"); 209 kilometres (130 mi), [Pamba](/wiki/Pamba_River "Pamba River"); 176 kilometres (109 mi), [Chaliyar](/wiki/Chaliyar_River "Chaliyar River"); 169 kilometres (105 mi), [Kadalundipuzha](/wiki/Kadalundipuzha_River "Kadalundipuzha River"); 130 kilometres (81 mi), [Chalakudipuzha](/wiki/Chalakudy_River "Chalakudy River"); 130 kilometres (81 mi), [Valapattanam](/wiki/Valapattanam_River "Valapattanam River"); 129 kilometres (80 mi) and the [Achankovil River](/wiki/Achankovil_River "Achankovil River"); 128 kilometres (80 mi). The average length of the rivers is 64 kilometres (40 mi). Many of the rivers are small and entirely fed by monsoon rain.[[134]](#cite_note-riverreport-135) As Kerala's rivers are small and lacking in [delta](/wiki/River_delta "River delta"), they are more prone to environmental effects. The rivers face problems such as sand mining and pollution.[[135]](#cite_note-Padmalal-136) The state experiences several natural hazards like landslides, floods and droughts. The state was also affected by the [2004 Indian Ocean tsunami](/wiki/2004_Indian_Ocean_tsunami "2004 Indian Ocean tsunami"),[[136]](#cite_note-Jha2010-137) and in 2018 received [the worst flooding](/wiki/2018_Kerala_floods "2018 Kerala floods") in nearly a century.[[137]](#cite_note-138) In 2024, Kerala experienced its worst [landslides](/wiki/2024_Wayanad_landslides "2024 Wayanad landslides") in history.[[138]](#cite_note-139)

### Climate

With around 120–140 rainy days per year,[[139]](#cite_note-Chacko_2002-140): 80 Kerala has a wet and maritime tropical climate influenced by the seasonal heavy rains of the [southwest summer monsoon](/wiki/Southwest_monsoon "Southwest monsoon") and [northeast winter monsoon](/wiki/Northeast_monsoon "Northeast monsoon").[[140]](#cite_note-Devrep-141) Around 65% of the rainfall occurs from June to August corresponding to the Southwest monsoon, and the rest from September to December corresponding to Northeast monsoon.[[140]](#cite_note-Devrep-141) The moisture-laden winds of the Southwest monsoon, on reaching the southernmost point of the [Indian Peninsula](/wiki/Indian_Peninsula "Indian Peninsula"), because of its topography, divides into two branches; the "Arabian Sea Branch" and the "Bay of Bengal Branch".[[141]](#cite_note-Jain-142) The "Arabian Sea Branch" of the Southwest monsoon first hits the Western Ghats,[[142]](#cite_note-Together_with_Social_Science_Term_II-143) making Kerala the first state in India to receive rain from the Southwest monsoon.[[143]](#cite_note-google6-144)[[144]](#cite_note-Aggarwal-145) The distribution of pressure patterns is reversed in the Northeast monsoon, during this season the cold winds from North India pick up moisture from the Bay of Bengal and precipitate it on the east coast of peninsular India.[[145]](#cite_note-google7-146)[[146]](#cite_note-Reddy-147) In Kerala, the influence of the Northeast monsoon is seen in southern districts only.[[147]](#cite_note-148) Kerala's rainfall averages 2,923 mm (115 in) annually.[[148]](#cite_note-IMD-149) Some of Kerala's drier lowland regions average only 1,250 mm (49 in); the mountains of the eastern [Idukki district](/wiki/Idukki_district "Idukki district") receive more than 5,000 mm (197 in) of [orographic precipitation](/wiki/Precipitation_(meteorology)#Orographic_precipitation "Precipitation (meteorology)"): the highest in the state. In eastern Kerala, a drier tropical wet and dry climate prevails. During the summer, the state is prone to gale-force winds, storm surges, cyclone-related torrential downpours, occasional droughts, and rises in sea level.[[149]](#cite_note-Brenkert_Malone_2003-150): 26, 46, 52 The mean daily temperature ranges from 19.8 °C to 36.7 °C.[[150]](#cite_note-moef-151) Mean annual temperatures range from 25.0 to 27.5 °C in the coastal lowlands to 20.0–22.5 °C in the eastern highlands.[[149]](#cite_note-Brenkert_Malone_2003-150): 65

### Flora and fauna

Main article: [Flora and fauna of Kerala](/wiki/Flora_and_fauna_of_Kerala "Flora and fauna of Kerala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Ichthyaetus_ichthyaetus_Kadalundi.jpg/250px-Ichthyaetus_ichthyaetus_Kadalundi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ichthyaetus_ichthyaetus_Kadalundi.jpg)

A migratory *[Ichthyaetus](/wiki/Ichthyaetus "Ichthyaetus")* in [Kadalundi Bird Sanctuary](/wiki/Kadalundi_Bird_Sanctuary "Kadalundi Bird Sanctuary")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Highland_Grassland_Shola_of_Silent_Valley_National_Park.jpg/250px-Highland_Grassland_Shola_of_Silent_Valley_National_Park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Highland_Grassland_Shola_of_Silent_Valley_National_Park.jpg)

[Silent Valley National Park](/wiki/Silent_Valley_National_Park "Silent Valley National Park")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Munnarhills.jpg/250px-Munnarhills.jpg)](/wiki/File:Munnarhills.jpg)

The [Cardamom Hills](/wiki/Cardamom_Hills "Cardamom Hills") are notable for [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity")

Most of the [biodiversity](/wiki/Biodiversity "Biodiversity") is concentrated and protected in the [Western Ghats](/wiki/Western_Ghats "Western Ghats"). Three-quarters of the land area of Kerala was under thick forest up to the 18th century.[[151]](#cite_note-History-152) As of 2004[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kerala&action=edit), over 25% of India's 15,000 plant species are in Kerala. Out of the 4,000 [flowering plant](/wiki/Flowering_plant "Flowering plant") species; 1,272 of which are [endemic](/wiki/Endemic_(ecology) "Endemic (ecology)") to Kerala, 900 are [medicinal](/wiki/Herbalism "Herbalism"), and 159 are [threatened](/wiki/Threatened_species "Threatened species").[[152]](#cite_note-Sreedharan_2004-153): 11 Its 9,400 km2 of forests include tropical wet evergreen and semi-evergreen forests (lower and middle elevations—3,470 km2), tropical moist and dry deciduous forests (mid-elevations—4,100 km2 and 100 km2, respectively), and montane subtropical and [temperate](/wiki/Temperate "Temperate") (*shola*) forests (highest elevations—100 km2). Altogether, 24% of Kerala is forested.[[152]](#cite_note-Sreedharan_2004-153): 12 Four of the world's [Ramsar Convention](/wiki/Ramsar_Convention "Ramsar Convention") listed [wetlands](/wiki/Wetland "Wetland")—[Lake Sasthamkotta](/wiki/Sasthamkotta_Lake "Sasthamkotta Lake"), [Ashtamudi Lake](/wiki/Ashtamudi_Lake "Ashtamudi Lake"), [Thrissur-Ponnani Kole Wetlands](/wiki/Thrissur-Ponnani_Kole_Wetlands "Thrissur-Ponnani Kole Wetlands"), and the Vembanad-Kol wetlands—are in Kerala,[[153]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018342-154) as well as 1455.4 km2 of the vast [Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Nilgiri_Biosphere_Reserve "Nilgiri Biosphere Reserve") and 1828 km2 of the [Agasthyamala Biosphere Reserve](/wiki/Agasthyamala_Biosphere_Reserve "Agasthyamala Biosphere Reserve").[[154]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018347-155) Subjected to extensive clearing for cultivation in the 20th century,[[155]](#cite_note-Jayarajan_2004-156): 6–7 much of the remaining forest cover is now protected from [clearfelling](/wiki/Clearfelling "Clearfelling").[[156]](#cite_note-Evans2008-157) Eastern Kerala's windward mountains shelter [tropical moist forests](/wiki/Tropical_and_subtropical_moist_broadleaf_forests "Tropical and subtropical moist broadleaf forests") and [tropical dry forests](/wiki/Tropical_and_subtropical_dry_broadleaf_forests "Tropical and subtropical dry broadleaf forests"), which are common in the Western Ghats.[[157]](#cite_note-google8-158)[[158]](#cite_note-Geer2008-159) The world's oldest teak plantation 'Conolly's Plot' is in [Nilambur](/wiki/Nilambur "Nilambur").[[159]](#cite_note-160)

Kerala's fauna are notable for their diversity and high rates of endemism: it includes 118 species of [mammals](/wiki/Mammal "Mammal") (1 endemic), [500 species of birds](/wiki/List_of_birds_of_Kerala "List of birds of Kerala"), 189 species of freshwater fish, 173 species of reptiles (10 of them endemic), and 151 species of [amphibians](/wiki/Amphibian "Amphibian") (36 endemic).[[160]](#cite_note-161) These are threatened by extensive habitat destruction, including soil erosion, landslides, [salinisation](/wiki/Soil_salinity "Soil salinity"), and resource extraction. In the forests, *sonokeling*, *[Dalbergia](/wiki/Dalbergia "Dalbergia") latifolia*, *anjili*, *mullumurikku*, *[Erythrina](/wiki/Erythrina "Erythrina")*, and *[Cassia](/wiki/Cinnamomum_cassia "Cinnamomum cassia")* number among the more than 1,000 species of trees in Kerala. Other plants include [bamboo](/wiki/Bamboo "Bamboo"), wild black pepper, wild [cardamom](/wiki/Cardamom "Cardamom"), the [calamus](/wiki/Calamus_(palm) "Calamus (palm)") [rattan](/wiki/Rattan "Rattan") palm, and aromatic vetiver grass, *Vetiveria zizanioides*.[[152]](#cite_note-Sreedharan_2004-153): 12 [Indian elephant](/wiki/Indian_elephant "Indian elephant"), [Bengal tiger](/wiki/Bengal_tiger "Bengal tiger"), [Indian leopard](/wiki/Indian_leopard "Indian leopard"), [Nilgiri tahr](/wiki/Nilgiri_tahr "Nilgiri tahr"), [common palm civet](/wiki/Common_palm_civet "Common palm civet"), and [grizzled giant squirrels](/wiki/Grizzled_giant_squirrel "Grizzled giant squirrel") are also found in the forests.[[152]](#cite_note-Sreedharan_2004-153): 12, 174–75 Reptiles include the [king cobra](/wiki/King_cobra "King cobra"), [viper](/wiki/Viperidae "Viperidae"), [python](/wiki/Pythonidae "Pythonidae"), and [mugger crocodile](/wiki/Mugger_crocodile "Mugger crocodile"). Kerala's birds include the [Malabar trogon](/wiki/Malabar_trogon "Malabar trogon"), the [great hornbill](/wiki/Great_hornbill "Great hornbill"), [Kerala laughingthrush](/wiki/Kerala_laughingthrush "Kerala laughingthrush"), [darter](/wiki/Darter "Darter") and [southern hill myna](/wiki/Southern_hill_myna "Southern hill myna"). In the lakes, wetlands, and waterways, fish such as [Kadu](/wiki/Heteropneustes_fossilis "Heteropneustes fossilis"), [Red Line Torpedo Barb](/wiki/Red_Line_Torpedo_Barb "Red Line Torpedo Barb") and *choottachi*; [orange chromide](/wiki/Orange_chromide "Orange chromide")—*Etroplus maculatus* are found.[[161]](#cite_note-162)[[152]](#cite_note-Sreedharan_2004-153): 163–65 Recently, a newly described tardigrade (water bears) species collected from Vadakara coast of Kerala named after Kerala State; *[Stygarctus keralensis](/wiki/Stygarctus_keralensis "Stygarctus keralensis")*.[[162]](#cite_note-163)

## Regions, districts and cities

Main articles: [Administrative divisions of Kerala](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Kerala "Administrative divisions of Kerala"), [Districts of Kerala](/wiki/Districts_of_Kerala "Districts of Kerala"), [List of revenue divisions of Kerala](/wiki/List_of_revenue_divisions_of_Kerala "List of revenue divisions of Kerala"), [List of taluks of Kerala](/wiki/List_of_taluks_of_Kerala "List of taluks of Kerala"), and [List of cities and towns in Kerala](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Kerala "List of cities and towns in Kerala")

See also: [Local government in Kerala](/wiki/Local_government_in_Kerala "Local government in Kerala")

The state's [14 districts](/wiki/List_of_districts_of_Kerala "List of districts of Kerala") are distributed among six regions: [North Malabar](/wiki/North_Malabar "North Malabar") (far-north Kerala), [South Malabar](/wiki/South_Malabar "South Malabar") (north-central Kerala), [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi") (central Kerala), [Northern Travancore](/wiki/Northern_Travancore "Northern Travancore") (south-central Kerala), [Central Travancore](/wiki/Central_Travancore "Central Travancore") (southern Kerala) and [Southern Travancore](/wiki/Southern_Travancore "Southern Travancore") (far-south Kerala). The districts that serve as administrative regions for taxation and land revenue purposes are further subdivided into [27 revenue divisions](/wiki/Revenue_Divisions_of_Kerala "Revenue Divisions of Kerala") and [77 taluks](/wiki/List_of_talukas_of_Kerala "List of talukas of Kerala"), which have fiscal and administrative powers over settlements within their borders, including maintenance of local land records. Kerala's taluks are further subdivided into 1,674 [revenue villages](/wiki/Revenue_Village "Revenue Village").[[163]](#cite_note-164)[[164]](#cite_note-165)

Since the [73rd](/wiki/List_of_amendments_of_the_Constitution_of_India "List of amendments of the Constitution of India") and [74th](/wiki/Seventy-fourth_Amendment_of_the_Constitution_of_India "Seventy-fourth Amendment of the Constitution of India") Constitutional Amendments, local self-government institutions in Kerala function as the third tier of government, comprising 14 [District Panchayats](/wiki/District_council_(India) "District council (India)"), 152 [Block Panchayats](/wiki/Panchayat_samiti "Panchayat samiti"), and 941 [Grama Panchayats](/wiki/Gram_panchayat "Gram panchayat") for rural governance, and 87 [Municipalities](/wiki/List_of_urban_local_bodies_in_Kerala "List of urban local bodies in Kerala"), six [Municipal Corporations](/wiki/List_of_municipal_corporations_in_Kerala "List of municipal corporations in Kerala") for urban governance.[[165]](#cite_note-166)

[Mahé](/wiki/Mah%C3%A9,_India "Mahé, India"), a part of the Indian [union territory](/wiki/Union_territory "Union territory") of [Puducherry](/wiki/Puducherry_(union_territory) "Puducherry (union territory)"),[[166]](#cite_note-167) though 647 kilometres (402 mi) away from it,[[167]](#cite_note-ShiraAssociates.2012-168) is a coastal exclave surrounded by Kerala on all of its landward approaches. The [Kannur District](/wiki/Kannur_district "Kannur district") surrounds Mahé on three sides with the [Kozhikode District](/wiki/Kozhikode_district "Kozhikode district") on the fourth.[[168]](#cite_note-Menon2002-169)

In 1664, the municipality of [Fort Kochi](/wiki/Fort_Kochi "Fort Kochi") was established by [Dutch Malabar](/wiki/Dutch_Malabar "Dutch Malabar"), making it the first municipality in the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), which was dissolved when the Dutch authority got weaker in the 18th century.[[169]](#cite_note-d_1664-170) The municipalities of [Kozhikode](/wiki/Kozhikode "Kozhikode"), [Palakkad](/wiki/Palakkad "Palakkad"), [Fort Kochi](/wiki/Fort_Kochi "Fort Kochi"), [Kannur](/wiki/Kannur "Kannur"), and [Thalassery](/wiki/Thalassery "Thalassery"), were founded on 1 November 1866[[170]](#cite_note-Ref1-171)[[171]](#cite_note-c1881-172)[[172]](#cite_note-google.co.in-173)[[121]](#cite_note-gazetteer-122) of the [British Indian Empire](/wiki/British_Indian_Empire "British Indian Empire"), making them the first modern municipalities in the state of Kerala. The Municipality of [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram "Thiruvananthapuram") came into existence in 1920. After two decades, during the reign of [Sree Chithira Thirunal](/wiki/Sree_Chithira_Thirunal "Sree Chithira Thirunal"), Thiruvananthapuram Municipality was converted into Corporation on 30 October 1940, making it the oldest Municipal Corporation of Kerala.[[173]](#cite_note-174) The first Municipal Corporation founded after the independence of [India](/wiki/India "India") as well as the second-oldest Municipal Corporation of the state is at [Kozhikode](/wiki/Kozhikode_Municipal_Corporation "Kozhikode Municipal Corporation") in the year 1962.[[174]](#cite_note-175) There are six [Municipal corporations](/wiki/Municipal_Corporations_in_India "Municipal Corporations in India") in Kerala that govern [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Municipal_Corporation "Thiruvananthapuram Municipal Corporation"), [Kozhikode](/wiki/Kozhikode_Municipal_Corporation "Kozhikode Municipal Corporation"), [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi_Municipal_Corporation "Kochi Municipal Corporation"), [Kollam](/wiki/Kollam_Municipal_Corporation "Kollam Municipal Corporation"), [Thrissur](/wiki/Thrissur_Municipal_Corporation "Thrissur Municipal Corporation"), and [Kannur](/wiki/Kannur_Municipal_Corporation "Kannur Municipal Corporation").[[175]](#cite_note-176) The [Thiruvananthapuram Municipal Corporation](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Municipal_Corporation "Thiruvananthapuram Municipal Corporation") is the largest corporation in Kerala while Kochi metropolitan area named [Kochi UA](/wiki/Kochi_UA "Kochi UA") is the largest urban agglomeration.[[176]](#cite_note-177) According to a survey by economics research firm Indicus Analytics in 2007, [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram "Thiruvananthapuram"), [Kozhikode](/wiki/Kozhikode "Kozhikode"), [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi"), [Kollam](/wiki/Kollam "Kollam"), [Thrissur](/wiki/Thrissur "Thrissur") are among the "best cities in India to live"; the survey used parameters such as health, education, environment, safety, public facilities and entertainment to rank the cities.[[177]](#cite_note-178)

The [Government Secretariat](/wiki/Kerala_Government_Secretariat "Kerala Government Secretariat") in [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram "Thiruvananthapuram") – the seat of executive administration of Kerala

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/High_Court_of_Kerala_Building.jpg/250px-High_Court_of_Kerala_Building.jpg)](/wiki/File:High_Court_of_Kerala_Building.jpg)

The [Kerala High Court](/wiki/Kerala_High_Court "Kerala High Court") complex in [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi")

The state is governed by a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") of [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy"). Kerala has a [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") legislature. The [Kerala Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Kerala_Legislative_Assembly "Kerala Legislative Assembly") also known as Niyamasabha, consists of 140 members who are elected for five-year terms.[[178]](#cite_note-179) The state elects 20 members to the [Lok Sabha](/wiki/Lok_Sabha "Lok Sabha"), the lower house of the [Indian Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_India "Parliament of India"), and 9 members to the [Rajya Sabha](/wiki/Rajya_Sabha "Rajya Sabha"), the upper house.[[179]](#cite_note-180)

The [Government of Kerala](/wiki/Government_of_Kerala "Government of Kerala") is a [democratically](/wiki/Democracy "Democracy") elected body in India with the [governor](/wiki/Governors_of_Kerala "Governors of Kerala") as its constitutional head and is appointed by the [president of India](/wiki/President_of_India "President of India") for a five-year term.[[180]](#cite_note-govern-181) The leader of the party or coalition with a majority in the [legislative assembly](/wiki/Kerala_Legislative_Assembly "Kerala Legislative Assembly") is appointed as the [chief minister](/wiki/List_of_chief_ministers_of_Kerala "List of chief ministers of Kerala") by the governor, and the [council of ministers](/wiki/Kerala_Council_of_Ministers "Kerala Council of Ministers") is appointed by the governor on the advice of the chief minister.[[180]](#cite_note-govern-181) The governor remains a ceremonial head of the state, while the chief minister and his council are responsible for day-to-day government functions. The council of ministers consists of cabinet ministers and ministers of state (MoS). The [secretariat](/wiki/Kerala_Government_Secretariat "Kerala Government Secretariat") headed by the [chief secretary](/wiki/Chief_secretary_(India) "Chief secretary (India)") assists the council of ministers in formulation and implementation of government policies. The chief secretary is the senior-most [civil servant](/wiki/Civil_service "Civil service") and the administrative head of the state government. Each government department is headed politically by a Minister and administratively by a Secretary to Government, in the rank of [additional chief secretar](/wiki/Additional_Chief_Secretary "Additional Chief Secretary"), [principal secretary](/wiki/Principal_secretary_(India) "Principal secretary (India)"), or [secretary](/wiki/Secretary "Secretary"), who is typically an officer of the [Indian Administrative Service](/wiki/Indian_Administrative_Service "Indian Administrative Service") (IAS). The additional chief secretaries/principal secretaries serve as the administrative heads of the department to which they are assigned. Each department also has officers of the rank of special secretary, additional secretary, joint secretary, deputy secretary, under secretary and other supporting officials. These secretariat departments are responsible for policy formulation and administrative supervision, while line departments are responsible for implementation.

Each Secretariat department is responsible for a number of line departments. The executive agencies (such as directorates, commissionerates, and similar entities) are responsible for implementing government policies and carrying out functions at the field level. These entities are headed by a Director, Commissioner, or similar authority, who functions as the Head of Department. Each of these departments has its own administrative subdivisions.[[181]](#cite_note-182)

The state is divided into fourteen districts, each headed by a [District Collector](/wiki/District_magistrate "District magistrate") appointed by the government. The District Collector is responsible for land revenue administration, disaster management, elections, maintenance of law and order, and coordination of various government departments within the district. For administrative convenience, each district is further divided into [revenue divisions](/wiki/List_of_revenue_divisions_of_Kerala "List of revenue divisions of Kerala"), [taluks](/wiki/Tehsil "Tehsil"), and [revenue villages](/wiki/Revenue_Village "Revenue Village"), primarily for the purpose of land revenue administration.[[182]](#cite_note-183)

The [judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary "Judiciary") consists of the [Kerala High Court](/wiki/Kerala_High_Court "Kerala High Court") and a system of lower courts.[[183]](#cite_note-184) The High Court, located in [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi"),[[184]](#cite_note-Congress_(US)2010-185) has a Chief Justice along with 35 permanent and twelve additional *[pro tempore](/wiki/Pro_tempore "Pro tempore")* justices as of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kerala&action=edit).[[185]](#cite_note-186) The high court also hears cases from the Union Territory of [Lakshadweep](/wiki/Lakshadweep "Lakshadweep").[[186]](#cite_note-Banerjea2002-187)[[187]](#cite_note-SharmaB.k.2007-188) The state's subordinate judiciary consists of District Courts in each of the 14 districts, headed by District Judges, along with civil courts (*Munsiff and Senior Civil Judges*) and criminal courts (*Judicial Magistrates, Chief Judicial Magistrates, and Sessions Courts*) handling cases according to their jurisdiction.

### Local government

Main article: [Local government in Kerala](/wiki/Local_government_in_Kerala "Local government in Kerala")

In Kerala, local government bodies such as Panchayats, Municipalities, and Corporations have existed since 1959. However, a significant decentralization initiative began in 1993, aligning with constitutional amendments by the [union government](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India").[[188]](#cite_note-Capdeck-189)[[189]](#cite_note-KILA-190): 12 The Kerala Panchayati Raj Act and the Kerala Municipality Act were enacted in 1994, establishing a three-tier system for rural governance and a single-tier system for urban governance.[[190]](#cite_note-191)[[191]](#cite_note-192)

The rural governance three-tier structure consists of [Gram Panchayats](/wiki/Gram_panchayat "Gram panchayat"), [Block Panchayats](/wiki/Panchayat_samiti "Panchayat samiti"), and [District Panchayats](/wiki/District_council_(India) "District council (India)").[[192]](#cite_note-TandonMohanty2006-193) The Kerala Panchayati Raj Act, 1994 define clear powers for these institutions.[[188]](#cite_note-Capdeck-189) For urban areas, the Kerala Municipality Act provides a single-tier system, comprising [Municipalities](/wiki/List_of_urban_local_bodies_in_Kerala "List of urban local bodies in Kerala") and [Municipal Corporations](/wiki/List_of_municipal_corporations_in_Kerala "List of municipal corporations in Kerala"), which are responsible for local governance and civic administration. These bodies are regularly constituted through [local body elections](/wiki/Elections_in_Kerala "Elections in Kerala").[[193]](#cite_note-Chaudhary2009-194) These bodies receive substantial administrative, legal, and financial powers to ensure effective decentralization.[[189]](#cite_note-KILA-190): 13 Currently, the state government allocates around 40% of the state plan outlay to local governments.[[194]](#cite_note-IsaacFranke2002-195) Kerala has 1,200 local bodies, including 941 Gram Panchayats, 152 Block Panchayats, 14 District Panchayats, 87 Municipalities, and 6 Municipal Corporations.[[195]](#cite_note-196)

Kerala was declared the first digital state of India in 2016 and, according to the India Corruption Survey 2019 by [Transparency International](/wiki/Transparency_International "Transparency International"), is considered the least corrupt state in India.[[196]](#cite_note-197)[[197]](#cite_note-198) The Public Affairs Index-2020 designated Kerala as the best-governed state in India.[[198]](#cite_note-199)

### Politics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Pinarayi_Vijayan_1.jpg/250px-Pinarayi_Vijayan_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pinarayi_Vijayan_1.jpg)

[Pinarayi Vijayan](/wiki/Pinarayi_Vijayan "Pinarayi Vijayan"),  
 12th [Chief Minister of Kerala](/wiki/Chief_Minister_of_Kerala "Chief Minister of Kerala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/VD_Satheesan.jpg/120px-VD_Satheesan.jpg)](/wiki/File:VD_Satheesan.jpg)

[V. D. Satheesan](/wiki/V._D._Satheesan "V. D. Satheesan"),  
[Leader of opposition](/wiki/List_of_leaders_of_the_opposition_in_the_Kerala_Legislative_Assembly "List of leaders of the opposition in the Kerala Legislative Assembly")

Kerala hosts two major political alliances: the [United Democratic Front](/wiki/United_Democratic_Front_(Kerala) "United Democratic Front (Kerala)") (UDF), led by the [Indian National Congress](/wiki/Indian_National_Congress "Indian National Congress"); and the [Left Democratic Front](/wiki/Left_Democratic_Front_(Kerala) "Left Democratic Front (Kerala)") (LDF), led by the [Communist Party of India (Marxist)](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_India_(Marxist) "Communist Party of India (Marxist)") (CPI(M)). As of[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kerala&action=edit) [2021 Kerala Legislative Assembly election](/wiki/2021_Kerala_Legislative_Assembly_election "2021 Kerala Legislative Assembly election"), the LDF is the ruling coalition; [Pinarayi Vijayan](/wiki/Pinarayi_Vijayan "Pinarayi Vijayan") of the Communist Party of India (Marxist) is the Chief Minister, while [V. D. Satheesan](/wiki/V._D._Satheesan "V. D. Satheesan") of the Indian National Congress is the [Leader of the Opposition](/wiki/List_of_leaders_of_the_opposition_in_the_Kerala_Legislative_Assembly "List of leaders of the opposition in the Kerala Legislative Assembly"). According to the [Constitution of India](/wiki/Constitution_of_India "Constitution of India"), Kerala has a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") of [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy"); [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage") is granted to residents.[[199]](#cite_note-200)

### Renaming to "Keralam"

There have been long-standing demands for changing the name to Keralam, the [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") name of the state. The [Kerala Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Kerala_Legislative_Assembly "Kerala Legislative Assembly") passed a unanimous resolution in June 2024 to change the state's name from "Kerala" to "Keralam," following an earlier unanimous resolution in August 2023. [Chief Minister](/wiki/Chief_Minister_of_Kerala "Chief Minister of Kerala") [Pinarayi Vijayan](/wiki/Pinarayi_Vijayan "Pinarayi Vijayan") had moved both resolutions, which sought to amend the state's name under Article 3 of the Constitution to reflect its Malayalam identity.[[23]](#cite_note-auto1-24)

The [Union Cabinet](/wiki/Union_Council_of_Ministers "Union Council of Ministers") approved this proposal on February 24, 2026. Following Cabinet approval, the [President of India](/wiki/President_of_India "President of India") will refer the Kerala (Alteration of Name) Bill, 2026 to the [State Legislative Assembly](/wiki/Kerala_Legislative_Assembly "Kerala Legislative Assembly") for expressing its views under the proviso to Article 3 of the Constitution of India.[[24]](#cite_note-auto-25)

After [Kerala Niyamasabha](/wiki/Kerala_Legislative_Assembly "Kerala Legislative Assembly")'s approval, this bill will be introduced in [Parliament of India](/wiki/Parliament_of_India "Parliament of India") and after being passed, the state's name will officially become Keralam.

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Kerala](/wiki/Economy_of_Kerala "Economy of Kerala")

After independence, the state was managed as a [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") [welfare economy](/wiki/Welfare_economy "Welfare economy").[[200]](#cite_note-201) The "Kerala phenomenon" or "[Kerala model](/wiki/Kerala_model "Kerala model") of development" of very high human development and in comparison low economic development has resulted from a strong service sector.[[149]](#cite_note-Brenkert_Malone_2003-150): 48[[201]](#cite_note-Tharamangalam_2005-202): 1 In 2019–20, the economy of Kerala was the [8th-largest](/wiki/List_of_Indian_states_and_union_territories_by_GDP "List of Indian states and union territories by GDP") in India with ₹8.55 trillion (US$100 billion) in gross state domestic product (GSDP) and a per capita net state domestic product of ₹222,000 (US$2,600).[[202]](#cite_note-203) In 2019–20, the [tertiary sector](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy") contributed around 63% of the state's [GSVA](/wiki/Gross_value_added "Gross value added"), compared to 28% by [secondary sector](/wiki/Secondary_sector_of_the_economy "Secondary sector of the economy"), and 8% by [primary sector](/wiki/Primary_sector_of_the_economy "Primary sector of the economy").[[203]](#cite_note-eco-204) In the period between 1960 and 2020, Kerala's economy was gradually shifting from an agrarian economy into a service-based one.[[203]](#cite_note-eco-204)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7c/Technopark_Trivandum_Ganga_and_Yamuna_Buildings.jpg/250px-Technopark_Trivandum_Ganga_and_Yamuna_Buildings.jpg)](/wiki/File:Technopark_Trivandum_Ganga_and_Yamuna_Buildings.jpg)

[Technopark](/wiki/Technopark,_Trivandrum "Technopark, Trivandrum") at [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram "Thiruvananthapuram"), the first and largest [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology") (IT) park in India

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Container_terminal.JPG/250px-Container_terminal.JPG)](/wiki/File:Container_terminal.JPG)

[Vallarpadam Terminal](/wiki/International_Container_Transshipment_Terminal,_Kochi "International Container Transshipment Terminal, Kochi") at [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi"), the first [transshipment terminal](/wiki/Container_terminal "Container terminal") in India

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Mappila_bay.JPG/250px-Mappila_bay.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mappila_bay.JPG)

[Mappila Bay](/wiki/Mappila_Bay "Mappila Bay") harbour at [Kannur](/wiki/Kannur "Kannur")

The state's [service sector](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy") which accounts for around 63% of its revenue is mainly based upon [hospitality industry](/wiki/Hospitality_industry "Hospitality industry"), [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Kerala "Tourism in Kerala"), [Ayurveda](/wiki/Ayurveda "Ayurveda") and medical services, pilgrimage, [information technology](/wiki/Information_technology "Information technology"), [transportation](/wiki/Roads_in_Kerala "Roads in Kerala"), financial sector, and [education](/wiki/Education_in_Kerala "Education in Kerala").[[204]](#cite_note-:15-205) Major initiatives under the industrial sector include [Cochin Shipyard](/wiki/Cochin_Shipyard "Cochin Shipyard"), shipbuilding, oil refinery, software industry, coastal mineral industries,[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018343-127) food processing, marine products processing, and Rubber based products. The primary sector of the state is mainly based upon [cash crops](/wiki/Cash_crop "Cash crop").[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018409-206) Kerala produces a significant amount of the national output of cash crops such as [coconut](/wiki/Coconut "Coconut"), [tea](/wiki/Tea "Tea"), [coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee"), [pepper](/wiki/Malabar_pepper "Malabar pepper"), [natural rubber](/wiki/Natural_rubber "Natural rubber"), [cardamom](/wiki/Cardamom "Cardamom"), and [cashew](/wiki/Cashew "Cashew") in India.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018409-206) The cultivation of food crops began to reduce since the 1950s.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018409-206)

Kerala's economy depends significantly on [emigrants working in foreign countries](/wiki/Kerala_Gulf_diaspora "Kerala Gulf diaspora"), mainly in the [Arab states of the Persian Gulf](/wiki/Arab_states_of_the_Persian_Gulf "Arab states of the Persian Gulf"), and the [remittances](/wiki/Remittance "Remittance") annually contribute more than a fifth of GSDP.[[206]](#cite_note-rem1-207) The state witnessed significant emigration during the [Gulf Boom](/wiki/Kerala_Gulf_diaspora#The_Gulf_Boom "Kerala Gulf diaspora") of the 1970s and early 1980s. In 2012, Kerala still received the highest remittances of all states: US$11.3 billion, which was nearly 16% of the US$71 billion remittances to the country.[[207]](#cite_note-208) In 2015, NRI deposits in Kerala have soared to over ₹1 lakh crore (US$12 billion), amounting to one-sixth of all the money deposited in NRI accounts, which comes to about ₹7 lakh crore (US$83 billion).[[208]](#cite_note-209) [Malappuram district](/wiki/Malappuram_district "Malappuram district") has the highest proportion of emigrant households in state.[[203]](#cite_note-eco-204) A study commissioned by the [Kerala State Planning Board](/wiki/Kerala_State_Planning_Board "Kerala State Planning Board"), suggested that the state look for other reliable sources of income, instead of relying on remittances to finance its expenditure.[[209]](#cite_note-Commission2008b-210)

As of March 2002, Kerala's banking sector comprised 3341 local branches: each branch served 10,000 people, lower than the national average of 16,000; the state has the third-highest bank penetration among Indian states.[[210]](#cite_note-RBI_2002-211) On 1 October 2011, Kerala became the first state in the country to have at least one banking facility in every village.[[211]](#cite_note-212) [Unemployment in 2007](/wiki/Unemployment_in_Kerala "Unemployment in Kerala") was estimated at 9.4%;[[212]](#cite_note-213) chronic issues are [underemployment](/wiki/Underemployment "Underemployment"), low employability of youth, and a low female [labour participation rate](/wiki/Labour_force "Labour force") of only 13.5%,[[213]](#cite_note-Nair_2004-214): 5, 13 as was the practice of *[Nokku kooli](/wiki/Nokku_kooli "Nokku kooli")*, "wages for looking on".[[214]](#cite_note-Outlook-2008-215) By 1999–2000, the rural and urban poverty rates dropped to 10.0% and 9.6%, respectively.[[215]](#cite_note-princeton6-216)

The state's budget of 2020–2021 was ₹1.15 lakh crore (US$14 billion).[[216]](#cite_note-Budget-217) The state government's tax revenues (excluding the shares from Union tax pool) amounted to ₹674 billion (US$8.0 billion) in 2020–21; up from ₹557 billion (US$6.6 billion) in 2019–20. Its [non-tax revenues](/wiki/Non-tax_revenue "Non-tax revenue") (excluding the shares from Union tax pool) of the Government of Kerala reached ₹146 billion (US$1.7 billion) in 2020–2021.[[216]](#cite_note-Budget-217) However, Kerala's high ratio of taxation to GSDP has not alleviated chronic budget deficits and unsustainable levels of government debt, which have impacted social services.[[217]](#cite_note-218) A record total of 223 [hartals](/wiki/Hartal "Hartal") were observed in 2006, resulting in a revenue loss of over ₹20 billion (US$240 million).[[218]](#cite_note-219) Kerala's 10% rise in GDP is 3% more than the national GDP. In 2013, capital expenditure rose 30% compared to the national average of 5%, owners of two-wheelers rose by 35% compared to the national rate of 15%, and the teacher-pupil ratio rose 50% from 2:100 to 4:100.[[219]](#cite_note-220)

The [Kerala Infrastructure Investment Fund Board](/wiki/Kerala_Infrastructure_Investment_Fund_Board "Kerala Infrastructure Investment Fund Board") is a government-owned [financial institution](/wiki/Financial_institution "Financial institution") in the state to mobilise funds for infrastructure development from outside the state revenue, aiming at overall infrastructure development of the state.[[220]](#cite_note-221)[[221]](#cite_note-222)
In November 2015, the [Ministry of Urban Development](/wiki/Ministry_of_Housing_and_Urban_Affairs "Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs") selected seven cities of Kerala for a [comprehensive development program](/wiki/List_of_government_schemes_in_India "List of government schemes in India") known as the [Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation](/wiki/Atal_Mission_for_Rejuvenation_and_Urban_Transformation "Atal Mission for Rejuvenation and Urban Transformation") (AMRUT).[[222]](#cite_note-223) A package of ₹2.5 million (US$30,000) was declared for each of the cities to develop service level improvement plan (SLIP), a plan for better functioning of the local urban bodies in the cities of Thiruvananthapuram, Kollam, Alappuzha, Kochi, Thrissur, Kozhikode, and Palakkad.[[223]](#cite_note-224) The [Grand Kerala Shopping Festival](/wiki/Grand_Kerala_Shopping_Festival "Grand Kerala Shopping Festival") (GKSF) was started in 2007, covering more than 3000 outlets across the nine cities of Kerala with huge tax discounts, VAT refunds and huge array of prizes.[[224]](#cite_note-GKSF_begins-225) [Lulu International Mall](/wiki/Lulu_Mall,_Thiruvananthapuram "Lulu Mall, Thiruvananthapuram") at [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram "Thiruvananthapuram") is the largest [shopping mall](/wiki/Shopping_Mall "Shopping Mall") in India.[[225]](#cite_note-226)

Despite many achievements, Kerala faces many challenges like high levels of unemployment that disproportionately impact educated women, a high degree of global exposure and a very fragile environment.[[226]](#cite_note-Hindu2021-227)

### Industries

Traditional industries manufacturing items; [coir](/wiki/Coir "Coir"), [handlooms](/wiki/Handloom "Handloom"), and [handicrafts](/wiki/Handicraft "Handicraft") employ around one million people.[[227]](#cite_note-KumarKerala2007-228) Kerala supplies 60% of the total global produce of white coir fibre. India's first coir factory was set up in [Alleppey](/wiki/Alleppey "Alleppey") in 1859–60.[[228]](#cite_note-229) The Central Coir Research Institute was established there in 1959.
