As per the 2006–2007 census by [SIDBI](/wiki/SIDBI "SIDBI"), there are 1,468,104 [micro, small and medium enterprises](/wiki/Small_and_medium_enterprises#India "Small and medium enterprises") in Kerala employing 3,031,272 people.[[229]](#cite_note-SIDBI_Report_2010-230)[[230]](#cite_note-231) The [KSIDC](/wiki/KSIDC "KSIDC") has promoted more than 650 medium and large manufacturing firms in Kerala, creating employment for 72,500 people.[[231]](#cite_note-232) A mining sector of 0.3% of GSDP involves extraction of [ilmenite](/wiki/Ilmenite "Ilmenite"), [kaolin](/wiki/Kaolin "Kaolin"), [bauxite](/wiki/Bauxite "Bauxite"), [silica](/wiki/Silica "Silica"), [quartz](/wiki/Quartz "Quartz"), [rutile](/wiki/Rutile "Rutile"), [zircon](/wiki/Zircon "Zircon"), and [sillimanite](/wiki/Sillimanite "Sillimanite").[[232]](#cite_note-GOK_2005c-233) Other major sectors are [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Kerala "Tourism in Kerala"), medical sector, [educational sector](/wiki/Education_in_Kerala "Education in Kerala"), banking, [ship building](/wiki/Cochin_shipyard "Cochin shipyard"), [oil refinery](/wiki/Kochi_Refineries "Kochi Refineries"), infrastructure, manufacturing, [home gardens](/wiki/Residential_garden "Residential garden"), animal husbandry and [business process outsourcing](/wiki/Business_process_outsourcing "Business process outsourcing").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Agriculture

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Tea_plantations_in_Munnar%2C_Kerala.jpg/250px-Tea_plantations_in_Munnar%2C_Kerala.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tea_plantations_in_Munnar,_Kerala.jpg)

Tea plantations at [Munnar](/wiki/Munnar "Munnar"), Kerala

[![A field](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/Nemmara-paddy.jpg/250px-Nemmara-paddy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nemmara-paddy.jpg)

A paddy field at [Palakkad](/wiki/Palakkad_district "Palakkad district"), also known as *The Granary of Kerala*

The major change in agriculture in Kerala occurred in the 1970s when production of rice fell due to increased availability of rice all over India and decreased availability of labour.[[233]](#cite_note-Sinha2003-234) Consequently, investment in rice production decreased and a major portion of the land shifted to the cultivation of perennial tree crops and seasonal crops.[[234]](#cite_note-Remesh2010-235)[[235]](#cite_note-Commission2008-236) Profitability of crops fell due to a shortage of farm labour, the high price of land, and the uneconomic size of operational holdings.[[236]](#cite_note-google9-237) Only 27.3% of the families in Kerala depend upon agriculture for their livelihood, which is also the least corresponding rate in India.[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018406-238)

Kerala produces 97% of the national output of black pepper[[238]](#cite_note-Limca_Book_of_Records-239) and accounts for 85% of the [natural rubber](/wiki/Natural_rubber "Natural rubber") in the country.[[239]](#cite_note-google10-240)[[240]](#cite_note-Economic_Affairs-241) [Coconut](/wiki/Coconut "Coconut"), [tea](/wiki/Tea "Tea"), [coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee"), [cashew](/wiki/Cashew "Cashew"), and spices—including cardamom, [vanilla](/wiki/Vanilla "Vanilla"), [cinnamon](/wiki/Cinnamon "Cinnamon"), and [nutmeg](/wiki/Nutmeg "Nutmeg") are the main agricultural products.[[111]](#cite_note-ChattopadhyayFranke2006-112): 74[[241]](#cite_note-Newton-242)[[242]](#cite_note-B.L.MarkoseMarkose2007-243)[[243]](#cite_note-Pradhan2009-244)[[244]](#cite_note-PradeepkumarPradeep2008-245)[[245]](#cite_note-OsellaOsella2000-246) Around 80% of [India](/wiki/India "India")'s export quality cashew kernels are prepared in [Kollam](/wiki/Kollam "Kollam").[[246]](#cite_note-247) The key cash crop is [coconut](/wiki/Coconut "Coconut") and Kerala ranks first in the area of coconut cultivation in India.[[247]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018407-248) Around 90% of the total [Cardamom](/wiki/Cardamom "Cardamom") produced in India is from Kerala.[[203]](#cite_note-eco-204) India is the second-largest producer of Cardamom in world.[[203]](#cite_note-eco-204) About 20% of the total [Coffee](/wiki/Coffee "Coffee") produced in India are from Kerala.[[205]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018409-206) The key agricultural staple is rice, with varieties grown in extensive paddy fields.[[248]](#cite_note-VarshneyRzóska1976-249) Home gardens made up a significant portion of the agricultural sector.[[249]](#cite_note-Dobbie2006-250)

### Fisheries

With 590 kilometres (370 [miles](/wiki/Mile "Mile")) of coastal belt,[[250]](#cite_note-251) 400,000 hectares of inland water resources[[251]](#cite_note-252) and approximately 220,000 active fishermen,[[252]](#cite_note-Commission1961-253) Kerala is one of the leading producers of fish in India.[[253]](#cite_note-google13-254) According to 2003–04 reports, about 11 lakh(1.1 million) people earn their livelihood from fishing and allied activities such as drying, processing, packaging, exporting and transporting fisheries. The annual yield of the sector was estimated as 6,08,000 tons in 2003–04.[[254]](#cite_note-255) This contributes to about 3% of the total economy of the state. In 2006, around 22% of the total Indian marine fishery yield was from Kerala.[[255]](#cite_note-Handbook-256) During the southwest monsoon, a suspended mud bank develops along the shore, which in turn leads to calm ocean water, peaking the output of the fishing industry. This phenomenon is locally called *[chakara](/wiki/Chakara "Chakara")*.[[256]](#cite_note-Gulati1984-257)[[257]](#cite_note-Journal_of_Kerala_Studies-258) The waters provide a large variety of fish: [pelagic species](/wiki/Pelagic_fish "Pelagic fish"); 59%, [demersal species](/wiki/Demersal_fish "Demersal fish"); 23%, [crustaceans](/wiki/Crustacean "Crustacean"), [molluscs](/wiki/Mollusca "Mollusca") and others for 18%.[[255]](#cite_note-Handbook-256) Around 1050,000 (1.050 million) fishermen haul an annual catch of 668,000 tonnes as of a 1999–2000 estimate; 222 fishing villages are strung along the 590-kilometre (370-mile) coast. Another 113 fishing villages dot the hinterland.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Transportation

### Roads

Main article: [Roads in Kerala](/wiki/Roads_in_Kerala "Roads in Kerala")

See also: [Kerala State Road Transport Corporation](/wiki/Kerala_State_Road_Transport_Corporation "Kerala State Road Transport Corporation")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Thrissur_sixlane.jpg/250px-Thrissur_sixlane.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thrissur_sixlane.jpg)

[NH 544](/wiki/National_Highway_544_(India) "National Highway 544 (India)") Six lane [Thrissur](/wiki/Thrissur "Thrissur") – [Vadakkencherry](/wiki/Vadakkencherry "Vadakkencherry")

Kerala has 331,904 kilometres (206,236 mi) of roads, which accounts for 5.6% of India's total.[[203]](#cite_note-eco-204)[[258]](#cite_note-road-259) This translates to about 9.94 kilometres (6.18 mi) of road per thousand people, compared to an average of 4.87 kilometres (3.03 mi) in the country.[[203]](#cite_note-eco-204)[[258]](#cite_note-road-259) Roads in Kerala include 1,812 kilometres (1,126 mi) of national highway; 1.6% of the nation's total, 4,342 kilometres (2,698 mi) of state highway; 2.5% of the nation's total, 27,470 kilometres (17,070 mi) of district roads; 4.7% of the nation's total, 33,201 kilometres (20,630 mi) of urban (municipal) roads; 6.3% of the nation's total, and 158,775 kilometres (98,658 mi) of rural roads; 3.8% of the nation's total.[[259]](#cite_note-:16-17-260) [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam_district "Kottayam district") has the maximum length of roads among the [districts of Kerala](/wiki/List_of_districts_of_Kerala "List of districts of Kerala"), while [Wayanad](/wiki/Wayanad "Wayanad") accounts for minimum.[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018422-261) Most of Kerala's west coast is accessible through the [NH 66](/wiki/National_Highway_66_(India) "National Highway 66 (India)") (previously NH 17 and 47); and the eastern side is accessible through state highways.[[261]](#cite_note-Kerala_PWD-262) New projects for hill and coastal highways were recently announced under [KIIFB](/wiki/Kerala_Infrastructure_Investment_Fund_Board "Kerala Infrastructure Investment Fund Board").[[262]](#cite_note-263) National Highway 66, with the longest stretch of road (1,622 kilometres (1,008 mi)) connects [Kanyakumari](/wiki/Kanyakumari "Kanyakumari") to [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai"); it enters Kerala via [Talapady](/wiki/Talapady "Talapady") in [Kasargod](/wiki/Kasargod "Kasargod") and passes through [Kannur](/wiki/Kannur "Kannur"), [Kozhikode](/wiki/Kozhikode "Kozhikode"), [Malappuram](/wiki/Malappuram "Malappuram"), [Guruvayur](/wiki/Guruvayur "Guruvayur"), [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi"), [Alappuzha](/wiki/Alappuzha "Alappuzha"), [Kollam](/wiki/Kollam "Kollam"), [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram "Thiruvananthapuram") before entering [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu").[[261]](#cite_note-Kerala_PWD-262) [Palakkad district](/wiki/Palakkad_district "Palakkad district") is generally referred to as the Gateway of Kerala, due to the presence of the Palakkad Gap in the Western Ghats, through which the northern ([Malabar](/wiki/Malabar_region "Malabar region")) and southern (Travancore) parts of Kerala are connected to the rest of India via road and rail. The state's largest checkpoint, [Walayar](/wiki/Walayar "Walayar"), is on [NH 544](/wiki/National_Highway_544_(India) "National Highway 544 (India)"), in the border town between Kerala and [Tamil Nadu](/wiki/Tamil_Nadu "Tamil Nadu"), through which a large amount of public and commercial transportation reaches the northern and central districts of Kerala.[[263]](#cite_note-264)

The [Department of Public Works](/wiki/Department_of_Public_Works_(Kerala) "Department of Public Works (Kerala)") is responsible for maintaining and expanding the [state highways system](/wiki/List_of_State_Highways_in_Kerala "List of State Highways in Kerala") and major district roads.[[264]](#cite_note-265) The Kerala State Transport Project (KSTP), which includes the [GIS](/wiki/GIS "GIS")-based Road Information and Management Project (RIMS), is responsible for maintaining and expanding the state highways in Kerala. It also oversees a few major district roads.[[265]](#cite_note-266)[[266]](#cite_note-267) Traffic in Kerala has been growing at a rate of 10–11% every year, resulting in high traffic and pressure on the roads. Traffic density is nearly four times the national average, reflecting the state's high population. Kerala's annual total of road accidents is among the nation's highest. The accidents are mainly the result of the narrow roads and irresponsible driving.[[267]](#cite_note-268) National Highways in Kerala are among the narrowest in the country and will remain so for the foreseeable future, as the state government has received an exemption that allows narrow national highways. In Kerala, highways are 45 metres (148 feet) wide. In other states, national highways are grade separated, 60 metres (200 feet) wide with a minimum of four lanes, as well as 6 or 8-lane access-controlled expressways.[[268]](#cite_note-269)[[269]](#cite_note-270) The National Highways Authority of India (NHAI) has threatened the Kerala state government that it will give higher priority to other states in highway development since political commitment to better highways in Kerala has been lacking.[[270]](#cite_note-271) As of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kerala&action=edit), Kerala had the highest road accident rate in the country, with most fatal accidents taking place along the state's national highways.[[271]](#cite_note-272)

### Railways

Main article: [List of Railway Stations in Kerala](/wiki/List_of_Railway_Stations_in_Kerala "List of Railway Stations in Kerala")

See also: [Kochi Metro](/wiki/Kochi_Metro "Kochi Metro")

[Southern Railway](/wiki/Southern_Railway_(India) "Southern Railway (India)") zone of [Indian Railways](/wiki/Indian_Railways "Indian Railways") operates all railway lines in the state connecting most major towns and cities except those in the highland districts of [Idukki](/wiki/Idukki_district "Idukki district") and [Wayanad](/wiki/Wayanad_district "Wayanad district").[[272]](#cite_note-273) The railway network in the state is controlled by two out of six divisions of the [Southern Railway](/wiki/Southern_Railway_zone "Southern Railway zone"); [Thiruvananthapuram Railway division](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Railway_division "Thiruvananthapuram Railway division") headquartered at [Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram "Thiruvananthapuram") and [Palakkad Railway Division](/wiki/Palakkad_Railway_Division "Palakkad Railway Division") headquartered at [Palakkad](/wiki/Palakkad "Palakkad").[[273]](#cite_note-274) [Thiruvananthapuram Central](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Central "Thiruvananthapuram Central") (TVC) is the busiest railway station in the state.[[274]](#cite_note-275) Kerala's major railway stations are:

* [Thiruvananthapuram Central](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_Central "Thiruvananthapuram Central") (TVC)
* [Ernakulam Junction (South)](/wiki/Ernakulam_Junction_railway_station "Ernakulam Junction railway station") (ERS)
* [Kozhikode](/wiki/Kozhikode_railway_station "Kozhikode railway station") (CLT)
* [Kollam Junction](/wiki/Kollam_Junction_railway_station "Kollam Junction railway station") (QLN)
* [Thrissur](/wiki/Thrissur_railway_station "Thrissur railway station") (TCR)
* [Palakkad Junction](/wiki/Palakkad_Junction "Palakkad Junction") (PGT)
* [Kannur](/wiki/Kannur_railway_station "Kannur railway station") (CAN)
* [Shoranur Junction](/wiki/Shornur_Junction "Shornur Junction") (SRR)
* [Ernakulam Town](/wiki/Ernakulam_Town "Ernakulam Town") (North) (ERN)
* [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam_railway_station "Kottayam railway station") (KTYM)
* [Chengannur](/wiki/Chengannur_railway_station "Chengannur railway station") (CNGR)
* [Alappuzha](/wiki/Alappuzha_railway_station "Alappuzha railway station") (ALLP)
* [Thiruvananthapuram North](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_North_Railway_station "Thiruvananthapuram North Railway station") (TVCN)
* [Kayamkulam](/wiki/Kayamkulam "Kayamkulam") Junction (KYJ)
* [Tirur](/wiki/Tirur_railway_station "Tirur railway station") (TIR)
* [Kasaragod](/wiki/Kasaragod_railway_station "Kasaragod railway station") (KGQ)
* [Aluva](/wiki/Aluva_railway_station "Aluva railway station") (AWY)
* [Thalassery](/wiki/Thalassery "Thalassery") (TLY)

### Airports

Main article: [List of airports in Kerala state](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Kerala_state "List of airports in Kerala state")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Cochin_International_Airport_Limited.jpg/250px-Cochin_International_Airport_Limited.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cochin_International_Airport_Limited.jpg)

[Cochin International Airport](/wiki/Cochin_International_Airport "Cochin International Airport"), the busiest airport in the state is also the first airport in the world to be fully powered by solar energy

Kerala has four international airports:

* [Thiruvananthapuram International Airport](/wiki/Thiruvananthapuram_International_Airport "Thiruvananthapuram International Airport")
* [Cochin International Airport](/wiki/Cochin_International_Airport "Cochin International Airport")
* [Calicut International Airport](/wiki/Calicut_International_Airport "Calicut International Airport")
* [Kannur International Airport](/wiki/Kannur_International_Airport "Kannur International Airport")

[Kollam Airport](/wiki/Kollam_Airport "Kollam Airport"), established under the Madras Presidency, but since closed, was the first airport in Kerala.[[275]](#cite_note-276) [Kannur](/wiki/Kannur "Kannur") had an airstrip used for commercial aviation as early as 1935 when [Tata](/wiki/Tata_Group "Tata Group") airlines operated weekly flights between [Mumbai](/wiki/Mumbai "Mumbai") and Thiruvananthapuram – stopping at [Goa](/wiki/Goa "Goa") and Kannur.[[276]](#cite_note-277) Trivandrum International Airport, managed by the [Airport Authority of India](/wiki/Airport_Authority_of_India "Airport Authority of India"), is among the oldest existing airports in South India. [Calicut International Airport](/wiki/Calicut_International_Airport "Calicut International Airport"), which was opened in 1988, is the second-oldest existing airport in Kerala and the oldest in the [Malabar region](/wiki/Malabar_District "Malabar District").[[277]](#cite_note-278) Cochin International Airport is the busiest in the state and the [seventh busiest](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_India "List of the busiest airports in India") in the country. It is also the first airport in the world to be fully powered by [solar energy](/wiki/CIAL_Solar_Power_Project "CIAL Solar Power Project")[[278]](#cite_note-BBC-279) and has won the coveted [Champion of the Earth](/wiki/Champions_of_the_Earth "Champions of the Earth") award, the highest environmental honour instituted by the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations").[[279]](#cite_note-UN-280) Cochin International Airport is also the first Indian airport to be incorporated as a [public limited company](/wiki/Public_limited_company "Public limited company"); it was funded by nearly 10,000 [non-resident Indians](/wiki/Non-resident_Indian_and_person_of_Indian_origin "Non-resident Indian and person of Indian origin") from 30 countries.[[280]](#cite_note-281)
Other than civilian airports, [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi") has a naval airport named [INS Garuda](/wiki/INS_Garuda "INS Garuda"). Thiruvananthapuram International Airport shares civilian facilities with the Southern Air Command of the [Indian Air Force](/wiki/Indian_Air_Force "Indian Air Force"). These facilities are used mostly by central government VIPs visiting Kerala.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Water transport

Main article: [Ports in Kerala](/wiki/Ports_in_Kerala "Ports in Kerala")

See also: [Kochi Water Metro](/wiki/Kochi_Water_Metro "Kochi Water Metro")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Cochin_Ship_Yard_Cranes.JPG/250px-Cochin_Ship_Yard_Cranes.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cochin_Ship_Yard_Cranes.JPG)

Cranes at the [Cochin Shipyard](/wiki/Cochin_Shipyard "Cochin Shipyard")

Kerala has [two major ports, four intermediate ports, and 13 minor ports](/wiki/Ports_in_Kerala "Ports in Kerala"), 4 of which have immigration checkpoint facilities.[[281]](#cite_note-282)[[282]](#cite_note-283) The major port in the state is at [Kochi](/wiki/Cochin_Port "Cochin Port"), which has an area of 8.27 km2.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018424-284) The [Vizhinjam International Seaport Thiruvananthapuram](/wiki/Vizhinjam_International_Seaport_Thiruvananthapuram "Vizhinjam International Seaport Thiruvananthapuram"), which is currently classified as a major port, only completed Phase I as others are under construction.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018424-284) Other intermediate ports include [Beypore](/wiki/Beypore "Beypore"), [Kollam](/wiki/Kollam "Kollam"), and [Azheekal](/wiki/Azhikode_and_Azhikkal "Azhikode and Azhikkal").[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018424-284) The remaining ports are classified as minor which include [Manjeshwaram](/wiki/Manjeshwaram "Manjeshwaram"), [Kasaragod](/wiki/Kasaragod "Kasaragod"), [Nileshwaram](/wiki/Nileshwaram "Nileshwaram"), [Kannur](/wiki/Kannur "Kannur"), [Thalassery](/wiki/Thalassery "Thalassery"), [Vadakara](/wiki/Vadakara "Vadakara"), [Ponnani](/wiki/Ponnani "Ponnani"), [Munambam](/wiki/Munambam "Munambam"), Manakodam, [Alappuzha](/wiki/Alappuzha "Alappuzha"), [Kayamkulam](/wiki/Kayamkulam "Kayamkulam"), [Neendakara](/wiki/Neendakara "Neendakara"), and [Valiyathura](/wiki/Valiyathura "Valiyathura").[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018424-284) The [Kerala Maritime Institute](/wiki/Kerala_Maritime_Institute "Kerala Maritime Institute") is headquartered at [Neendakara](/wiki/Neendakara "Neendakara"), which has an additional subcentre at [Kodungallur](/wiki/Kodungallur "Kodungallur") too.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018424-284) The state has numerous [backwaters](/wiki/Kerala_backwaters "Kerala backwaters"), which are used for commercial [inland navigation](/wiki/Inland_navigation "Inland navigation"). Transport services are mainly provided by country craft and passenger vessels. There are 67 navigable rivers in the state while the total length of inland waterways is 1,687 kilometres (1,048 mi).[[284]](#cite_note-google14-285) The main constraints to the expansion of inland navigation are; lack of depth in waterways caused by silting, lack of maintenance of navigation systems and bank protection, accelerated growth of the [water hyacinth](/wiki/Water_hyacinth "Water hyacinth"), lack of modern inland craft terminals, and lack of a cargo handling system.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Kerala also have India's first water metro , called 'Kochi water metro'.[[285]](#cite_note-286) The Kochi water metro project identified 15 routes, connecting 10 islands along a network of routes that span 78 km with a fleet of 78 fast, and fully electrically propelled hybrid ferries .[[286]](#cite_note-287)

The 616 kilometres (383 mi) long West-Coast Canal is the longest waterway in state connecting [Kasaragod](/wiki/Kasaragod "Kasaragod") to [Poovar](/wiki/Poovar "Poovar").[[287]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018423-288) It is divided into five sections: 41 kilometres (25 mi) long [Kasaragod](/wiki/Kasaragod "Kasaragod")-[Nileshwaram](/wiki/Nileshwaram "Nileshwaram") reach, 188 kilometres (117 mi) long [Nileshwaram](/wiki/Nileshwaram "Nileshwaram")-[Kozhikode](/wiki/Kozhikode "Kozhikode") reach, 160 kilometres (99 mi) [Kozhikode](/wiki/Kozhikode "Kozhikode")-[Kottapuram](/wiki/Kottappuram,_Thrissur "Kottappuram, Thrissur") reach, 168 kilometres (104 mi) long [National Waterway 3](/wiki/National_Waterway_3 "National Waterway 3") ([Kottapuram](/wiki/Kottappuram,_Thrissur "Kottappuram, Thrissur")-[Kollam](/wiki/Kollam "Kollam") reach), and 74 kilometres (46 mi) long [Kollam](/wiki/Kollam "Kollam")-[Vizhinjam](/wiki/Vizhinjam "Vizhinjam") reach.[[203]](#cite_note-eco-204) The [Conolly Canal](/wiki/Conolly_Canal "Conolly Canal"), which is a part of the West-Coast Canal, connects the city of [Kozhikode](/wiki/Kozhikode "Kozhikode") with [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi") through [Ponnani](/wiki/Ponnani "Ponnani"), passing through the districts of [Malappuram](/wiki/Malappuram_district "Malappuram district") and [Thrissur](/wiki/Thrissur_district "Thrissur district"). It begins at [Vadakara](/wiki/Vadakara "Vadakara").[[288]](#cite_note-hindu_jan08-289) It was constructed in the year 1848 under the orders of then [District collector](/wiki/District_collector "District collector") of [Malabar](/wiki/Malabar_District "Malabar District"), H. V. Conolly, initially to facilitate movement of goods to [Kallayi](/wiki/Kallayi "Kallayi") Port from the hinterlands of Malabar through [Kuttiady](/wiki/Kuttiady "Kuttiady") and [Korapuzha](/wiki/Korapuzha "Korapuzha") river systems.[[288]](#cite_note-hindu_jan08-289) It was the main waterway for the cargo movement between Kozhikode and Kochi through [Ponnani](/wiki/Ponnani "Ponnani"), for more than a century.[[288]](#cite_note-hindu_jan08-289) Other important waterways in Kerala include the [Alappuzha](/wiki/Alappuzha "Alappuzha")-[Changanassery](/wiki/Changanassery "Changanassery") Canal, [Alappuzha](/wiki/Alappuzha "Alappuzha")-[Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam")-[Athirampuzha](/wiki/Athirampuzha "Athirampuzha") Canal, and [Kottayam](/wiki/Kottayam "Kottayam")-[Vaikom](/wiki/Vaikom "Vaikom") Canal.[[283]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018424-284)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Kerala](/wiki/Demographics_of_Kerala "Demographics of Kerala")

See also: [Ethnic groups in Kerala](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Kerala "Ethnic groups in Kerala") and [List of people from Kerala](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Kerala "List of people from Kerala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Kerala_Population_Pyramid_in_5-year_age_groups_%282011_census%29.png/250px-Kerala_Population_Pyramid_in_5-year_age_groups_%282011_census%29.png)](/wiki/File:Kerala_Population_Pyramid_in_5-year_age_groups_(2011_census).png)

The [Population pyramid](/wiki/Population_pyramid "Population pyramid") of Kerala

Kerala is home to 2.8% of India's population; with a density of 859 persons per km2, its land is nearly three times as densely settled as the national average of 370 persons per km2.[[289]](#cite_note-Census_2011-290) As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kerala&action=edit), Thiruvananthapuram is the most populous city in Kerala.[[290]](#cite_note-Census2011cities-291) In the state, the rate of population growth is India's lowest, and the decadal growth of 4.9% in 2011 is less than one-third of the all-India average of 17.6%.[[289]](#cite_note-Census_2011-290) Kerala's population more than doubled between 1951 and 1991 by adding 15.6 million people to reach 29.1 million residents in 1991; the population stood at 33.3 million by 2011.[[289]](#cite_note-Census_2011-290) Kerala's coastal regions are the most densely settled with population of 2022 persons per km2, 2.5 times the overall population density of the state, 859 persons per km2, leaving the eastern hills and mountains comparatively sparsely populated.[[291]](#cite_note-kerala_fact_sheet-292) Kerala is the [second-most urbanised major state](/wiki/Urbanisation_in_India "Urbanisation in India") in the country with [47.7% urban population](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Kerala_by_urban_area_growth "List of cities in Kerala by urban area growth") according to the [2011 Census of India](/wiki/2011_Census_of_India "2011 Census of India").[[292]](#cite_note-ubn-293) Around 31.8 million Keralites are predominantly Malayali.[[289]](#cite_note-Census_2011-290) The state's 321,000 indigenous tribal *[Adivasis](/wiki/Adivasi "Adivasi")*, 1.1% of the population, are concentrated in the east.[[293]](#cite_note-Kalathil_2004-294): 10–12

### Gender

There is a tradition of matrilineal inheritance in Kerala, where the mother is the head of the household.[[294]](#cite_note-295) As a result, women in Kerala have had a much higher standing and influence in the society. This was common among certain influential castes and is a factor in the value placed on daughters. Christian missionaries also influenced Malayali women in that they started schools for girls from poor families.[[295]](#cite_note-296) Opportunities for women such as education and gainful employment often translate into a lower birth rate,[[296]](#cite_note-297) which in turn, make education and employment more likely to be accessible and more beneficial for women. This creates an upward spiral for both the women and children of the community that is passed on to future generations. According to the Human Development Report of 1996, Kerala's [Gender Development Index](/wiki/Gender_Development_Index "Gender Development Index") was 597; higher than any other state of India. Factors, such as high rates of female literacy, education, work participation and life expectancy, along with favourable [sex ratio](/wiki/Human_sex_ratio "Human sex ratio"), contributed to it.[[297]](#cite_note-Joseph-298)

Kerala's sex ratio of 1.084 (females to males) is higher than that of the rest of India; it is the only state where women outnumber men.[[201]](#cite_note-Tharamangalam_2005-202): 2 While having the opportunities that education affords them, such as political participation, keeping up to date with current events, reading religious texts, etc., these tools have still not translated into full, [equal rights](/wiki/Women%27s_rights "Women's rights") for the women of Kerala. There is a general attitude that women must be restricted for their own benefit. In the state, despite the social progress, gender still influences [social mobility](/wiki/Social_mobility "Social mobility").[[298]](#cite_note-299)[[299]](#cite_note-300)[[300]](#cite_note-301)

#### LGBT rights

Main article: [LGBT rights in Kerala](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Kerala "LGBT rights in Kerala")

Kerala has been at the forefront of LGBT issues in [India](/wiki/India "India").[[301]](#cite_note-302) Kerala is one of the first [states in India](/wiki/States_and_union_territories_of_India "States and union territories of India") to form a welfare policy for the transgender community. In 2016, the Kerala government introduced free [sex reassignment surgery](/wiki/Sex_reassignment_surgery "Sex reassignment surgery") through government hospitals.[[302]](#cite_note-303)[[303]](#cite_note-304)[[304]](#cite_note-305) [Queerala](/wiki/Queerala "Queerala") is one of the major LGBT organisations in Kerala. It campaigns for increased awareness of LGBT people and sensitisation concerning healthcare services, workplace policies and educational curriculum.[[305]](#cite_note-306) Since 2010, [Kerala Queer Pride](/wiki/Kerala_Queer_Pride "Kerala Queer Pride") has been held annually across various cities in Kerala.[[306]](#cite_note-307)

In June 2019, the Kerala government passed a new order that members of the transgender community should not be referred to as the "[third gender](/wiki/Third_gender "Third gender")" or "other gender" in government communications. Instead, the term "transgender" should be used. Previously, the gender preferences provided in government forms and documents included male, female, and other/third gender.[[307]](#cite_note-308)[[308]](#cite_note-309)

### Human Development Index

See also: [Kerala Model](/wiki/Kerala_Model "Kerala Model")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/2006_Human_Development_Index_for_India_map_by_states%2C_HDI_data_by_GoI_and_UNDP_India.svg/250px-2006_Human_Development_Index_for_India_map_by_states%2C_HDI_data_by_GoI_and_UNDP_India.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2006_Human_Development_Index_for_India_map_by_states,_HDI_data_by_GoI_and_UNDP_India.svg)

[Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") map for Indian states in 2006, as calculated by [Government of India](/wiki/Government_of_India "Government of India") and [United Nations Development Programme](/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Programme "United Nations Development Programme").[[309]](#cite_note-310)

Under a democratic communist local government, Kerala has achieved a record of social development much more advanced than the Indian average.[[310]](#cite_note-311) As of 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kerala&action=edit), Kerala has a [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") (HDI) of 0.770, which is in the "high" category, ranking it first in the country.[[311]](#cite_note-snhdi-gdl-312) It was 0.790 in 2007–08[[312]](#cite_note-IDHR_2011-313) and it had a consumption-based HDI of 0.920, which is better than that of many developed countries.[[312]](#cite_note-IDHR_2011-313) Comparatively higher spending by the government on [primary level education](/wiki/Primary_education "Primary education"), [health care](/wiki/Health_care "Health care") and the elimination of [poverty](/wiki/Poverty_in_India "Poverty in India") from the 19th century onwards has helped the state maintain an exceptionally high HDI;[[313]](#cite_note-UNDP2005-314)[[314]](#cite_note-UNDP20054-315) the report was prepared by the central government's [Institute of Applied Manpower Research](/wiki/Institute_of_Applied_Manpower_Research "Institute of Applied Manpower Research").[[315]](#cite_note-hdi_kerala_chart-316)[[316]](#cite_note-hdi_kerala-317) However, the Human Development Report 2005, prepared by Centre for Development Studies envisages a virtuous phase of inclusive development for the state since the advancement in human development had already started aiding the economic development of the state.[[313]](#cite_note-UNDP2005-314) Kerala is also widely regarded as the [cleanest](/wiki/Public_hygiene "Public hygiene") and healthiest state in India.[[317]](#cite_note-318)

According to the 2011 census, Kerala has the highest [literacy rate](/wiki/Literacy_rate "Literacy rate") (94%) among Indian states. In 2018, the literacy rate was calculated to be 96.2% in the 2018 literacy survey conducted by the National Statistical Office, India.[[318]](#cite_note-Literacy_survey_2018-319) In the [Kottayam district](/wiki/Kottayam_district "Kottayam district"), the literacy rate was 97%.[[319]](#cite_note-320)[[11]](#cite_note-pc-census2011-11)[[320]](#cite_note-IBNLiteracy2013-321) The [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") in Kerala is 74 years, among the highest in India as of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kerala&action=edit).[[321]](#cite_note-322) Kerala's rural poverty rate fell from 59% (1973–1974) to 12% (1999–2010); the overall (urban and rural) rate fell 47% between the 1970s and 2000s against the 29% fall in overall poverty rate in India.[[322]](#cite_note-323) By 1999–2000, the rural and urban poverty rates dropped to 10.0% and 9.6%, respectively.[[215]](#cite_note-princeton6-216) The 2013 Tendulkar Committee Report on poverty estimated that the percentages of the population living below the poverty line in rural and urban Kerala are 9.1% and 5.0%, respectively.[[323]](#cite_note-324) These changes stem largely from efforts begun in the late 19th century by the kingdoms of Cochin and Travancore to boost social welfare.[[324]](#cite_note-325)[[325]](#cite_note-326) This focus was maintained by Kerala's post-independence government.[[149]](#cite_note-Brenkert_Malone_2003-150)[[326]](#cite_note-Varma_2005-327): 48 Kerala is the [least impoverished state](/wiki/List_of_Indian_states_and_union_territories_by_poverty_rate "List of Indian states and union territories by poverty rate") in India according to [NITI Aayog](/wiki/NITI_Aayog "NITI Aayog")'s Sustainable Development Goals dashboard and [Reserve Bank of India](/wiki/Reserve_Bank_of_India "Reserve Bank of India")'s *Handbook of Statistics on Indian Economy*.[[327]](#cite_note-328)[[328]](#cite_note-329)

Kerala has undergone a "[demographic transition](/wiki/Demographic_transition "Demographic transition")" characteristic of such [developed nations](/wiki/Developed_nation "Developed nation") as [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), and [Norway](/wiki/Norway "Norway").[[201]](#cite_note-Tharamangalam_2005-202): 1 In 2005, 11.2% of people were over the age of 60.[[326]](#cite_note-Varma_2005-327) In 2023, the BBC reported on the problems and benefits which have arisen from migration away from Kerala, focussing on the village of Kumbanad.[[329]](#cite_note-330)

In 2004, the birthrate was low at 18 per 1,000.[[330]](#cite_note-Kutty_2004_6-331) According to the 2011 census, Kerala had a total fertility rate (TFR) of 1.6. All districts except Malappuram district had fertility rates below 2. Fertility rate is highest in [Malappuram district](/wiki/Malappuram_district "Malappuram district") (2.2) and lowest in [Pathanamthitta district](/wiki/Pathanamthitta_district "Pathanamthitta district") (1.3).[[331]](#cite_note-332) In 2001, Muslims had the TFR of 2.6 as against 1.5 for Hindus and 1.7 for Christians.[[332]](#cite_note-333) The state also is regarded as the "least corrupt Indian state" according to the surveys conducted by CMS Indian Corruption Study (CMS-ICS)[[333]](#cite_note-334) Transparency International (2005)[[334]](#cite_note-335) and *India Today* (1997).[[335]](#cite_note-336) Kerala has the lowest [homicide](/wiki/Homicide "Homicide") rate among Indian states, with 1.1 per 100,000 in 2011.[[336]](#cite_note-ncrb-337) In respect of female empowerment, some negative factors such as higher [suicide rate](/wiki/Suicide_rate "Suicide rate"), lower share of [earned income](/wiki/Earned_income "Earned income"), [child marriage](/wiki/Child_marriage "Child marriage"),[[337]](#cite_note-338) complaints of [sexual harassment](/wiki/Sexual_harassment "Sexual harassment") and limited freedom are reported.[[297]](#cite_note-Joseph-298) The child marriage is lower in Kerala. The Malappuram district has the highest number of child marriages and the number of such cases is increasing in Malappuram. Child marriages are particularly [higher among the Muslim community](/wiki/Child_marriage_among_Muslims_in_Kerala "Child marriage among Muslims in Kerala").[[338]](#cite_note-339)[[339]](#cite_note-340) In 2019, Kerala recorded the highest child sex abuse complaints in India.[[340]](#cite_note-341)

In 2015, Kerala had the highest conviction rate of any state, over 77%.[[341]](#cite_note-342) Kerala has the lowest proportion of homeless people in rural India, <0.1%,[[342]](#cite_note-343) and the state is attempting to reach the goal of becoming the first "Zero Homeless State", in addition to its acclaimed "Zero landless project", with private organisations and the expatriate Malayali community funding projects for building homes for the homeless.[[343]](#cite_note-344) The state was also among the lowest in the [India State Hunger Index](/wiki/India_State_Hunger_Index "India State Hunger Index") next only to [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab,_India "Punjab, India"). In 2015 Kerala became the first "complete digital state" by implementing e-governance initiatives.[[344]](#cite_note-345)

### Healthcare

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Regional_Cancer_Centre%2C_Trivandrum.jpg/250px-Regional_Cancer_Centre%2C_Trivandrum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Regional_Cancer_Centre,_Trivandrum.jpg)

[Regional Cancer Centre, Trivandrum](/wiki/Regional_Cancer_Centre,_Trivandrum "Regional Cancer Centre, Trivandrum")

See also: [List of government hospitals in Kerala](/wiki/List_of_government_hospitals_in_Kerala "List of government hospitals in Kerala")

Kerala is a pioneer in implementing the [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") program.[[345]](#cite_note-346) The [sub-replacement fertility](/wiki/Sub-replacement_fertility "Sub-replacement fertility") level and [infant mortality rate](/wiki/Infant_mortality_rate "Infant mortality rate") are lower compared to those of other states, estimated from 12[[149]](#cite_note-Brenkert_Malone_2003-150)[[330]](#cite_note-Kutty_2004_6-331): 49 to 14[[346]](#cite_note-Krishnaswami_2004-347): 5 deaths per 1,000 live births; as per the National Family Health Survey 2015–16, it has dropped to 6.[[347]](#cite_note-348) According to a study commissioned by [Lien](/wiki/Lien_Ying_Chow "Lien Ying Chow") Foundation, a [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore")-based philanthropic organisation, Kerala is considered to be the best place to die in India based on the state's provision of palliative care for patients with serious illnesses.[[348]](#cite_note-349) However, Kerala's [morbidity rate](/wiki/Morbidity_rate "Morbidity rate") is higher than that of any other Indian state—118 (rural) and 88 (urban) per 1,000 people. The corresponding figures for all India were 55 and 54 per 1,000, respectively as of 2005[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kerala&action=edit).[[346]](#cite_note-Krishnaswami_2004-347): 5 Kerala's 13.3% [prevalence](/wiki/Prevalence "Prevalence") of [low birth weight](/wiki/Low_birth_weight "Low birth weight") is higher than that of many [first world](/wiki/First_world "First world") nations.[[330]](#cite_note-Kutty_2004_6-331) Outbreaks of [water-borne diseases](/wiki/Water-borne_disease "Water-borne disease") such as [diarrhoea](/wiki/Diarrhoea "Diarrhoea"), [dysentery](/wiki/Dysentery "Dysentery"), [hepatitis](/wiki/Hepatitis "Hepatitis"), and [typhoid](/wiki/Typhoid "Typhoid") among the more than 50% of people who rely on 3 million [water wells](/wiki/Water_well "Water well") is an issue worsened by the lack of [sewers](/wiki/Sanitary_sewer "Sanitary sewer").[[349]](#cite_note-Roy_2004-350): 5–7 As of 2017, the state has the highest number of diabetes patients and also the highest prevalence rate of the disease in India.[[350]](#cite_note-351)

The [United Nations Children's Fund](/wiki/United_Nations_Children%27s_Fund "United Nations Children's Fund") (UNICEF) and the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") designated Kerala the world's first "[baby-friendly](/wiki/Baby_Friendly_Hospital_Initiative "Baby Friendly Hospital Initiative") state" because of its effective promotion of breastfeeding over formulas.[[351]](#cite_note-352)[[352]](#cite_note-353) Over 95% of Keralite births are hospital-delivered and the state also has the lowest infant mortality rate in the country. The third National Family Health Survey ranks Kerala first in "Institutional Delivery" with 100% of births being in medical facilities.[[291]](#cite_note-kerala_fact_sheet-292) [Ayurveda](/wiki/Ayurveda "Ayurveda"),[[353]](#cite_note-Unnikrishnan_2004-354): 13 *[siddha](/wiki/Siddha "Siddha")*, and endangered and endemic modes of [traditional medicine](/wiki/Traditional_medicine "Traditional medicine"), including *kalari*, *marmachikitsa* and *vishavaidyam*, are practised. Some occupational communities such as [Kaniyar](/wiki/Kaniyar "Kaniyar") were known as native medicine men in relation to the practice of such streams of medical systems, apart from their traditional vocation.[[354]](#cite_note-355) These propagate via *[gurukula](/wiki/Gurukula "Gurukula")* discipleship,[[353]](#cite_note-Unnikrishnan_2004-354): 5–6 and comprise a fusion of both medicinal and alternative treatments.[[353]](#cite_note-Unnikrishnan_2004-354): 15 The *[Arya Vaidya Sala](/wiki/Arya_Vaidya_Sala "Arya Vaidya Sala")* established by [Vaidyaratnam P. S. Warrier](/wiki/Vaidyaratnam_P._S._Warrier "Vaidyaratnam P. S. Warrier") at [Kottakkal](/wiki/Kottakkal "Kottakkal") (about 10 km from [Malappuram](/wiki/Malappuram "Malappuram")) in 1902, is the largest Ayurvedic medicinal network and health centre in the state.[[355]](#cite_note-Warrier-356)[[356]](#cite_note-Arya_Vaidya_Sala_Kottakkal_–_Part_1-357)[[357]](#cite_note-mal-358) It is also one of the largest Ayurvedic medicinal brands in the world.[[355]](#cite_note-Warrier-356)[[356]](#cite_note-Arya_Vaidya_Sala_Kottakkal_–_Part_1-357)[[357]](#cite_note-mal-358)

In 2014, Kerala became the first state in India to offer free cancer treatment to the poor, via a program called Sukrutham.[[358]](#cite_note-359) People in Kerala experience elevated incidence of [cancers](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer"), [liver](/wiki/Liver_disease "Liver disease") and [kidney](/wiki/Kidney_disease "Kidney disease") diseases.[[359]](#cite_note-360) In April 2016, the *Economic Times* reported that 250,000 residents undergo treatment for cancer. It also reported that approximately 150 to 200 liver transplants are conducted in the region's hospitals annually. Approximately 42,000 cancer cases are reported in the region annually. This is believed to be an underestimate as private hospitals may not be reporting their figures. Long waiting lists for kidney donations have stimulated illegal trade in human kidneys, and prompted the establishment of the Kidney Federation of India which aims to support financially disadvantaged patients.[[360]](#cite_note-361) As of 2017–18, there are 6,691 modern medicine institutions under the [Department of Health Services](/wiki/Department_of_Health_and_Family_Welfare_(Kerala) "Department of Health and Family Welfare (Kerala)"), of which the total bed strength is 37,843; 15,780 in rural areas and 22,063 in urban.[[361]](#cite_note-dhs-362)

### Language

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8b/Language_Map_of_Kerala_%282011_Census%29.svg/250px-Language_Map_of_Kerala_%282011_Census%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Language_Map_of_Kerala_(2011_Census).svg)

Languages spoken by district:  

|  |
| --- |
| **Malayalam** 80–90%  90–100% |

[Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") is the official language of Kerala and one of the [Classical languages of India](/wiki/Languages_of_India "Languages of India").[[362]](#cite_note-thehindu20130524-363)[[363]](#cite_note-364) There is a significant [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") population throughout Kerala mainly in [Idukki district](/wiki/Idukki_district "Idukki district") and [Palakkad district](/wiki/Palakkad_district "Palakkad district") in which it accounts for 17.48% and 4.8% respectively of the two districts' populations.[[364]](#cite_note-lg-365) [Tulu](/wiki/Tulu_language "Tulu language") and [Kannada](/wiki/Kannada "Kannada") are spoken mainly in the northern parts of [Kasaragod district](/wiki/Kasaragod_district "Kasaragod district"), each of which account for 8.77% and 4.23% of total population in the district, respectively.[[364]](#cite_note-lg-365)[[365]](#cite_note-366)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Kerala](/wiki/Religion_in_Kerala "Religion in Kerala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Jain_temple%2C_Wayanad_IMG_3494_by_Joseph_Lazer.jpg/250px-Jain_temple%2C_Wayanad_IMG_3494_by_Joseph_Lazer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jain_temple,_Wayanad_IMG_3494_by_Joseph_Lazer.jpg)

[Jain Temple](/wiki/Jain_Temple,_Kidanganad "Jain Temple, Kidanganad") at [Sultan Bathery](/wiki/Sultan_Bathery "Sultan Bathery").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Cheraman_juma_masjid_Old.jpg/250px-Cheraman_juma_masjid_Old.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cheraman_juma_masjid_Old.jpg)

Model of original [Cheraman Juma Mosque](/wiki/Cheraman_Juma_Mosque "Cheraman Juma Mosque").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Palayoor_St._Thomas_Syro-Malabar_Church.jpg/250px-Palayoor_St._Thomas_Syro-Malabar_Church.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palayoor_St._Thomas_Syro-Malabar_Church.jpg)

[St. Thomas Syro-Malabar Church, Palayoor](/wiki/St._Thomas_Syro-Malabar_Church,_Palayoor "St. Thomas Syro-Malabar Church, Palayoor").

**Religion in Kerala** (2011)[[366]](#cite_note-census_2016_religion-367)

1. [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Kerala "Hinduism in Kerala") (54.7%)
2. [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Kerala "Islam in Kerala") (26.6%)
3. [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Kerala "Christianity in Kerala") (18.4%)
4. Other or none (0.32%)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Kerala_religions_2011.png/250px-Kerala_religions_2011.png)](/wiki/File:Kerala_religions_2011.png)

Percentage of the most popular religion in each Taluk of Kerala

Kerala is very religiously diverse with [Hindus](/wiki/Hindus "Hindus"), [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") and [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians") having a significant population throughout the state. Kerala is often regarded as one of the most diverse states in all of India.[[367]](#cite_note-368)[[368]](#cite_note-369) [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") is the most widely professed faith in Kerala, with significant numbers of adherents to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") and [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"). In comparison with the rest of India, Kerala experiences relatively little [sectarianism](/wiki/Sectarianism "Sectarianism").[[369]](#cite_note-370) According to [2011 Census of India](/wiki/2011_Census_of_India "2011 Census of India") figures, 54.7% of Kerala's residents are Hindus, 26.6% are Muslims, 18.4% are Christians, and the remaining 0.3% follow another religion or have no religious affiliation.[[370]](#cite_note-censusindia2011religion-371) Hindus represent the biggest religious group in all districts except [Malappuram](/wiki/Malappuram_district "Malappuram district"), where they are outnumbered by Muslims.[[371]](#cite_note-thehindu1-372) Kerala has the largest population of Christians in India.[[372]](#cite_note-373) As of 2016, Hindus, Muslims, Christians and others account for 41.9%, 42.6%, 15.4% and 0.2% of the total childbirths in the state, respectively.[[373]](#cite_note-374)

Islam arrived in Kerala, a part of the larger [Indian Ocean](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean") rim, via spice and silk traders from the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East"). Historians do not rule out the possibility of Islam being introduced to Kerala as early as the seventh century CE.[[374]](#cite_note-indiatimes3-375)[[375]](#cite_note-autogenerated20002-376) Notable has been the occurrence of [Cheraman Perumal Tajuddin](/wiki/Legend_of_Cheraman_Perumals "Legend of Cheraman Perumals"), the mythical Hindu king who moved to [Arabia](/wiki/Arabia "Arabia") to meet [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") and converted to Islam.[[376]](#cite_note-377)[[377]](#cite_note-378)[[378]](#cite_note-379) Kerala Muslims are generally referred to as the [Mappilas](/wiki/Malabar_Muslims "Malabar Muslims"). Mappilas are but one among the many communities that form the Muslim population of Kerala.[[379]](#cite_note-KunhaliV2-380)[[380]](#cite_note-Divakaruni20112-381) According to the [Legend of Cheraman Perumals](/wiki/Legend_of_Cheraman_Perumals "Legend of Cheraman Perumals"), the first Indian mosque was built in 624 CE at [Kodungallur](/wiki/Kodungallur "Kodungallur") with the mandate of the last the ruler (the Cheraman Perumal) of [Chera dynasty](/wiki/Chera_dynasty "Chera dynasty"), who converted to Islam during the lifetime of [Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad") (c. 570–632).[[381]](#cite_note-382)[[382]](#cite_note-SimpsonKresse2008-383)[[383]](#cite_note-Kupferschmidt1987-384)[[384]](#cite_note-Raṇṭattāṇi2007-385)

Ancient Christian tradition says that Christianity reached the shores of Kerala in 52 CE with the arrival of [Thomas the Apostle](/wiki/Thomas_the_Apostle "Thomas the Apostle"), one of the [Twelve Apostles](/wiki/Twelve_Apostles "Twelve Apostles") of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") Christ.[[385]](#cite_note-386)[[386]](#cite_note-387)[[387]](#cite_note-Encyclopædia_Britannica-388)[[388]](#cite_note-Menachery-389) [Saint Thomas Christians](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Christians "Saint Thomas Christians") include [Syro-Malabar Catholic](/wiki/Syro-Malabar_Church "Syro-Malabar Church"),[[389]](#cite_note-Ponnumuthan1996-390) [Syro-Malankara Catholic](/wiki/Syro-Malankara_Catholic_Church "Syro-Malankara Catholic Church"),[[390]](#cite_note-Williams1996-391) [Jacobite Syrian Christian Church](/wiki/Jacobite_Syrian_Christian_Church "Jacobite Syrian Christian Church"),[[391]](#cite_note-AndersonTang2005-392) [Mar Thoma Syrian Church](/wiki/Mar_Thoma_Syrian_Church "Mar Thoma Syrian Church"),[[392]](#cite_note-393) [Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church](/wiki/Malankara_Orthodox_Syrian_Church "Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church"),[[393]](#cite_note-Russell2010-394) the [Syrian Anglicans](/wiki/Saint_Thomas_Anglicans "Saint Thomas Anglicans") of the [CSI](/wiki/Church_of_South_India "Church of South India")[[394]](#cite_note-395) and [Pentecostal Saint Thomas Christians](/wiki/Pentecostal_Saint_Thomas_Christians "Pentecostal Saint Thomas Christians").[[395]](#cite_note-396) The origin of the [Latin Catholic](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") Christians in Kerala is the result of the missionary endeavours of the Portuguese [Padroado](/wiki/Padroado "Padroado") in the 16th century.[[396]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-397)[[397]](#cite_note-RakRaj2008-398)[[398]](#cite_note-Subramanian2009-399) As a consequence of centuries of mixing with colonial immigrants, beginning with the Portuguese, Dutch, French, British and other Europeans, there is a community of [Anglo-Indians](/wiki/Anglo-Indians "Anglo-Indians") in Kerala of mixed European and Indian parentage or ancestry. Kerala has the highest population of Christians among all the states of India.[[399]](#cite_note-400)

[Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") reached Kerala in the 10th century BCE during the time of [King Solomon](/wiki/Solomon "Solomon").[[400]](#cite_note-401) They are called [Cochin Jews](/wiki/Cochin_Jews "Cochin Jews") or Malabar Jews and are the oldest group of [Jews in India](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_India "History of the Jews in India").[[401]](#cite_note-402)[[402]](#cite_note-403) There was a significant Jewish community which existed in Kerala until the 20th century, when most of them [migrated to Israel](/wiki/Aliyah "Aliyah").[[403]](#cite_note-Roland1998-404) The [Paradesi Synagogue](/wiki/Cochin_Synagogue "Cochin Synagogue") at [Kochi](/wiki/Kochi "Kochi") is the oldest synagogue in the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_Nations "Commonwealth Nations").[[404]](#cite_note-405) [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism_in_Kerala "Jainism in Kerala") has a considerable following in the [Wayanad district](/wiki/Wayanad_district "Wayanad district").[[405]](#cite_note-LockieCarpenter2012-406)[[406]](#cite_note-MathewBaviskar2009-407)

## Education

Main article: [Education in Kerala](/wiki/Education_in_Kerala "Education in Kerala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/University_of_Kerala.jpg/250px-University_of_Kerala.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Kerala.jpg)

[University of Kerala](/wiki/University_of_Kerala "University of Kerala") at Thiruvananthapuram

The [Kerala school of astronomy and mathematics](/wiki/Kerala_school_of_astronomy_and_mathematics "Kerala school of astronomy and mathematics") flourished between the 14th and 16th centuries. In attempting to solve astronomical problems, the Kerala school independently created a number of important mathematics concepts, including [series expansion](/wiki/Series_expansion "Series expansion") for trigonometric functions.[[407]](#cite_note-roy-408)[[408]](#cite_note-409) In the early decades of the 19th century, the modern educational transformation of Kerala was triggered by the efforts of the [Church Mission Society](/wiki/Church_Mission_Society "Church Mission Society") missionaries to promote mass education.[[409]](#cite_note-410)[[410]](#cite_note-411)[[411]](#cite_note-412)[[412]](#cite_note-413)[[413]](#cite_note-414) Following the recommendations of the [Wood's despatch](/wiki/Wood%27s_despatch "Wood's despatch") of 1854, the princely states of Travancore and Cochin launched mass education drives mainly based on castes and communities, and introduced a system of [grant-in-aid](/wiki/Grant-in-aid "Grant-in-aid") to attract more private initiatives.[[414]](#cite_note-Devrep53-58-415) Catholic institutions such as [St Thomas College Thrissur](/wiki/St._Thomas_College,_Thrissur "St. Thomas College, Thrissur") and [SB College Changanasserry](/wiki/St._Berchmans_College "St. Berchmans College") were established under the leadership of the Catholic Church. The efforts by leaders such as [Fr. Kuriakose Elias Chavara](/wiki/Kuriakose_Elias_Chavara "Kuriakose Elias Chavara"), [Mar Charles Lavigne SJ](/wiki/Charles_Lavigne "Charles Lavigne"), [Vaikunda Swami](/wiki/Ayya_Vaikundar "Ayya Vaikundar"), [Narayana Guru](/wiki/Narayana_Guru "Narayana Guru") and [Ayyankali](/wiki/Ayyankali "Ayyankali") in aiding the socially discriminated castes in the state—with the help of community-based organisations like [Nair Service Society](/wiki/Nair_Service_Society "Nair Service Society"), [SNDP](/wiki/Sree_Narayana_Trust "Sree Narayana Trust"), [Muslim Educational Society](/wiki/Muslim_Educational_Society "Muslim Educational Society"), Muslim Mahajana Sabha, Yoga Kshema Sabha (of Nambudiris) and congregations of Christian churches—led to the further development of mass education in Kerala.[[414]](#cite_note-Devrep53-58-415)

In 1991, Kerala became the first state in India to be recognised as completely literate, although the effective literacy rate at that time was only 90%.[[415]](#cite_note-Soundarapandian2000-416) In 2006–2007, the state topped the Education Development Index (EDI) of the 21 major states in India.[[416]](#cite_note-417) As of 2007[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Kerala&action=edit), enrolment in elementary education was almost 100%; and, unlike other states in India, educational opportunity was almost equally distributed among sexes, social groups, and regions.[[417]](#cite_note-Devrep255-258-418) According to the 2011 census, Kerala has a 93.9% literacy, compared to the national literacy rate of 74.0%.[[320]](#cite_note-IBNLiteracy2013-321) In January 2016, Kerala became the first Indian state to achieve 100% primary education through its *Athulyam* literacy programme.[[418]](#cite_note-419)

The educational system prevailing in the state's schools specifies an initial 10-year course of study, which is divided into three stages: lower primary, upper primary, and secondary school—known as *4+3+3*, which signifies the number of years for each stage.[[417]](#cite_note-Devrep255-258-418) After the first 10 years of schooling, students typically enroll in [Higher Secondary Schooling](/wiki/Junior_college "Junior college") in one of the three major streams—[liberal arts](/wiki/Liberal_arts "Liberal arts"), commerce, or science.[[419]](#cite_note-Edu_Ker-420) The majority of public schools are affiliated with the [Kerala Board of Public Examination](/wiki/Kerala_Board_of_Public_Examination "Kerala Board of Public Examination").[[420]](#cite_note-421) Other educational boards are the [Indian Certificate of Secondary Education](/wiki/Indian_Certificate_of_Secondary_Education "Indian Certificate of Secondary Education") (ICSE), the [Central Board for Secondary Education](/wiki/Central_Board_for_Secondary_Education "Central Board for Secondary Education") (CBSE), and the [National Institute of Open Schooling](/wiki/National_Institute_of_Open_Schooling "National Institute of Open Schooling") (NIOS).[[419]](#cite_note-Edu_Ker-420)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Kerala](/wiki/Culture_of_Kerala "Culture of Kerala")

[![Kathakali Performance](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Kathakali_BNC.jpg/250px-Kathakali_BNC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kathakali_BNC.jpg)

A [Kathakali](/wiki/Kathakali "Kathakali") artist

[![Pookkalam](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/5_image_collage_of_floral_arrangement_during_the_Hindu_festival_of_Onam_Kerala.jpg/250px-5_image_collage_of_floral_arrangement_during_the_Hindu_festival_of_Onam_Kerala.jpg)](/wiki/File:5_image_collage_of_floral_arrangement_during_the_Hindu_festival_of_Onam_Kerala.jpg)

During [Onam](/wiki/Onam "Onam"), Kerala's biggest celebration, Keralites create *pookkalam* (floral carpet) designs in front of their houses.

[![Thrissur Pooram festival](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/ThrissurPooram-Kuda.jpg/250px-ThrissurPooram-Kuda.jpg)](/wiki/File:ThrissurPooram-Kuda.jpg)

[Thrissur Pooram](/wiki/Thrissur_Pooram "Thrissur Pooram") festival

[![Mohiniattam](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Mohiniyattam_at_Kerala_School_Kalolsavam_2019_02.jpg/250px-Mohiniyattam_at_Kerala_School_Kalolsavam_2019_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mohiniyattam_at_Kerala_School_Kalolsavam_2019_02.jpg)
