[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Sadhya_DSW.jpg/250px-Sadhya_DSW.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sadhya_DSW.jpg)

Onam [Sadya](/wiki/Sadya "Sadya")

The culture of Kerala is composite and cosmopolitan in nature and it is an integral part of [Indian culture](/wiki/Indian_culture "Indian culture").[[421]](#cite_note-Menon3-422) It is a synthesis of [Aryan](/wiki/Indo-Aryan_peoples "Indo-Aryan peoples"), [Dravidian](/wiki/South_Indian_culture "South Indian culture"), [Arab](/wiki/Arab_culture "Arab culture"), and [European](/wiki/Culture_of_Europe "Culture of Europe") cultures,[[422]](#cite_note-423) developed over millennia, under influences from other parts of India and abroad.[[423]](#cite_note-Menon1978a-424) It is defined by its antiquity and the organic continuity sustained by the [Malayali people](/wiki/Malayali_people "Malayali people").[[424]](#cite_note-425) It was elaborated through centuries of contact with neighbouring and overseas cultures.[[425]](#cite_note-Bhagyalekshmy_2004d_29-426) However, the geographical insularity of Kerala from the rest of the country has resulted in the development of a distinctive lifestyle, art, architecture, language, literature and social institutions.[[421]](#cite_note-Menon3-422) Over 10,000 festivals are celebrated in the state every year.[[426]](#cite_note-1000hrs-427) The [Malayalam calendar](/wiki/Malayalam_calendar "Malayalam calendar"), a solar sidereal calendar started from 825 CE in Kerala,[[427]](#cite_note-Devika2005-428) finds common usage in planning agricultural and religious activities.[[428]](#cite_note-Singh2004-429) [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam"), one of the classical languages in India, is Kerala's [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language").[[429]](#cite_note-Benedikter2009-430) Over a dozen other [scheduled and unscheduled languages](/wiki/Languages_with_official_status_in_India "Languages with official status in India") are also spoken.[[430]](#cite_note-census2011-langreport-431) Kerala has the greatest consumption of alcohol in India.[[431]](#cite_note-432)

### Festivals

Main articles: [Temple Festivals of Kerala](/wiki/Temple_Festivals_of_Kerala "Temple Festivals of Kerala") and [Onam](/wiki/Onam "Onam")

Many of the temples in Kerala hold festivals on specific days of the year.[[432]](#cite_note-433) A common characteristic of these festivals is the hoisting of a holy flag which is brought down on the final day of the festival after immersing the deity.[[433]](#cite_note-434) Some festivals include Poorams, the best known of these being the [Thrissur Pooram](/wiki/Thrissur_Pooram "Thrissur Pooram").[[434]](#cite_note-435) "Elephants, firework displays and huge crowds" are the major attractions of Thrissur Pooram.[[435]](#cite_note-436) Other known festivals are [Makaravilakku](/wiki/Makaravilakku "Makaravilakku"),[[436]](#cite_note-Ltd.2013b-437) [Chinakkathoor Pooram](/wiki/Chinakkathoor_Pooram "Chinakkathoor Pooram"), [Attukal Pongala](/wiki/Attukal_Pongala "Attukal Pongala") and [Nenmara Vallangi Vela](/wiki/Nenmara_Vallangi_Vela "Nenmara Vallangi Vela")[[437]](#cite_note-Stott2014-438) Other than these, festivals locally known as [utsavams](/wiki/Utsavam "Utsavam") are conducted by many temples mostly on annual basis. Temples that can afford it will usually involve at least one richly caparisoned elephant as part of the festivities. The idol in the temple is taken out on a procession around the countryside atop this elephant. When the procession visits homes around the temple, people will usually present rice, coconuts, and other offerings to it.[[438]](#cite_note-NarayananKurup1976-439) Processions often include traditional music such as [Panchari melam](/wiki/Panchari_melam "Panchari melam") or [Panchavadyam](/wiki/Panchavadyam "Panchavadyam").[[439]](#cite_note-Killius2006-440) [Eid al-Fitr](/wiki/Eid_al-Fitr "Eid al-Fitr") and [Eid al-Adha](/wiki/Eid_al-Adha "Eid al-Adha") are celebrated by the Muslim community of the state while the festivals like [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas") and [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter") are observed by the Christians.[[440]](#cite_note-Malabar-441) Onam is a harvest festival celebrated by the people of Kerala and is reminiscent of the state's agrarian past.[[441]](#cite_note-Choondal1980-442)[[442]](#cite_note-Menon2007b-443) It is a local festival of Kerala[[443]](#cite_note-Darpan2006b-444) consisting of a four-day public holidays; from Onam Eve (Uthradam) to the fourth Onam Day.[[444]](#cite_note-445) Onam falls in the Malayalam month of Chingam (August–September)[[445]](#cite_note-446) and marks the commemoration of the homecoming of King [Mahabali](/wiki/Mahabali "Mahabali").[[446]](#cite_note-MythOnam-447) The total duration of Onam is 10 days and it is celebrated all across Kerala. It is one of the festivals celebrated with cultural elements such as [Vallam Kali](/wiki/Vallam_Kali "Vallam Kali"),[[447]](#cite_note-448) [Pulikali](/wiki/Pulikali "Pulikali"),[[448]](#cite_note-Ltd.2013-449) Pookkalam,[[449]](#cite_note-450) Thumbi Thullal[[450]](#cite_note-Mohapatra2013-451) and [Onavillu](/wiki/Onavillu "Onavillu").[[451]](#cite_note-(Princess.)1998-452)

### Music and dance

Main articles: [Arts of Kerala](/wiki/Arts_of_Kerala "Arts of Kerala") and [Music of Kerala](/wiki/Music_of_Kerala "Music of Kerala")

Kerala is home to a number of [performance arts](/wiki/Performance_art "Performance art"). These include five classical dance forms: [Kathakali](/wiki/Kathakali "Kathakali"), [Mohiniyattam](/wiki/Mohiniyattam "Mohiniyattam"), [Koodiyattom](/wiki/Koodiyattom "Koodiyattom"), [Thullal](/wiki/Ottan_thullal "Ottan thullal") and [Krishnanattam](/wiki/Krishnanattam "Krishnanattam"), which originated and developed in the temple theatres during the classical period under the patronage of royal houses.[[452]](#cite_note-453) [Kerala natanam](/wiki/Kerala_natanam "Kerala natanam"), [Thirayattam](/wiki/Thirayattam "Thirayattam"),[[453]](#cite_note-454) [Kaliyattam](/wiki/Kaliyattam "Kaliyattam"), [Theyyam](/wiki/Theyyam "Theyyam"), [Koothu](/wiki/Koothu "Koothu") and [Padayani](/wiki/Padayani "Padayani") are other dance forms associated with the temple culture of the region.[[454]](#cite_note-Menon2008-455) Some traditional dance forms such as [Oppana](/wiki/Oppana "Oppana") and [Duffmuttu](/wiki/Duffmuttu "Duffmuttu") were popular among the Muslims of the state,[[455]](#cite_note-google19-456) while [Margamkali](/wiki/Margamkali "Margamkali") and [Parichamuttukali](/wiki/Parichamuttukali "Parichamuttukali") are popular among the Syrian Christians and [Chavittu nadakom](/wiki/Chavittu_nadakom "Chavittu nadakom") is popular among the Latin Christians.[[456]](#cite_note-Nārāyaṇappaṇikkar1991-457)[[457]](#cite_note-Kasbekar2006-458) The development of classical music in Kerala is attributed to the contributions it received from the traditional performance arts associated with the temple culture of Kerala.[[458]](#cite_note-Menon2-459) The development of the indigenous classical music form, [Sopana Sangeetham](/wiki/Sopana_Sangeetham "Sopana Sangeetham"), illustrates the rich contribution that temple culture has made to the arts of Kerala.[[458]](#cite_note-Menon2-459) [Carnatic music](/wiki/Carnatic_music "Carnatic music") dominates Keralite traditional music. This was the result of [Swathi Thirunal Rama Varma](/wiki/Swathi_Thirunal_Rama_Varma "Swathi Thirunal Rama Varma")'s popularisation of the genre in the 19th century.[[425]](#cite_note-Bhagyalekshmy_2004d_29-426) Raga-based renditions known as *sopanam* accompany *kathakali* performances.[[459]](#cite_note-SchechnerAppel1990-460) *Melam*, including the *paandi* and *panchari* variants, is a more percussive style of music;[[460]](#cite_note-BroughtonEllingham2000-461) it is performed at *[Kshetram](/wiki/Hindu_temple "Hindu temple")*-centered festivals using the *[chenda](/wiki/Chenda "Chenda")*. *Panchavadyam* is a form of percussion ensemble, in which artists use five types of percussion instruments.[[460]](#cite_note-BroughtonEllingham2000-461) Kerala's visual arts range from [traditional murals](/wiki/Murals_of_Kerala "Murals of Kerala") to the works of [Raja Ravi Varma](/wiki/Raja_Ravi_Varma "Raja Ravi Varma"), the state's most renowned painter.[[458]](#cite_note-Menon2-459) Most of the castes and communities in Kerala have rich collections of folk songs and ballads associated with a variety of themes; *[Vadakkan Pattukal](/wiki/Vadakkan_Pattukal "Vadakkan Pattukal")* (Northern Ballads), *Thekkan pattukal* (Southern Ballads), *Vanchi pattukal* (Boat Songs), *[Mappila Pattukal](/wiki/Mappila_Pattukal "Mappila Pattukal")* (Muslim songs) and *Pallipattukal* (Church songs) are a few of them.[[461]](#cite_note-462)

### Cinema

Main article: [Malayalam cinema](/wiki/Malayalam_cinema "Malayalam cinema")

[Malayalam films](/wiki/Malayalam_Cinema "Malayalam Cinema") carved a niche for themselves in the Indian film industry with the presentation of social themes.[[462]](#cite_note-Menon-463)[[463]](#cite_note-464) Directors from Kerala, like [Adoor Gopalakrishnan](/wiki/Adoor_Gopalakrishnan "Adoor Gopalakrishnan"), [Mankada Ravi Varma](/wiki/Mankada_Ravi_Varma "Mankada Ravi Varma"), [G. Aravindan](/wiki/G._Aravindan "G. Aravindan"), [Bharathan](/wiki/Bharathan "Bharathan"), [P. Padmarajan](/wiki/P._Padmarajan "P. Padmarajan"), [M.T. Vasudevan Nair](/wiki/M.T.Vasudevan_Nair "M.T.Vasudevan Nair"), [K.G. George](/wiki/K.G._George "K.G. George"), [Priyadarshan](/wiki/Priyadarshan "Priyadarshan"), [John Abraham](/wiki/John_Abraham_(director) "John Abraham (director)"), [Ramu Karyat](/wiki/Ramu_Karyat "Ramu Karyat"), [K S Sethumadhavan](/wiki/K_S_Sethumadhavan "K S Sethumadhavan"), [A. Vincent](/wiki/A._Vincent "A. Vincent") and [Shaji N Karun](/wiki/Shaji_N_Karun "Shaji N Karun") have made a considerable contribution to the Indian [parallel cinema](/wiki/Parallel_cinema "Parallel cinema"). Kerala has also given birth to numerous actors, such as [Mohanlal](/wiki/Mohanlal "Mohanlal"), [Mammootty](/wiki/Mammootty "Mammootty"), [Satyan](/wiki/Satyan "Satyan"), [Prem Nazir](/wiki/Prem_Nazir "Prem Nazir"), [Madhu](/wiki/Madhu_(actor) "Madhu (actor)"), [Sheela](/wiki/Sheela "Sheela"), [Sharada](/wiki/Sharada_(actress) "Sharada (actress)"), [Miss Kumari](/wiki/Miss_Kumari "Miss Kumari"), [Jayan](/wiki/Jayan "Jayan"), [Adoor Bhasi](/wiki/Adoor_Bhasi "Adoor Bhasi"), [Seema](/wiki/Seema_(actress) "Seema (actress)"), [Bharath Gopi](/wiki/Bharath_Gopi "Bharath Gopi"), [Thilakan](/wiki/Thilakan "Thilakan"), [Vijaya Raghavan](/wiki/Vijaya_Raghavan "Vijaya Raghavan"), [Kalabhavan Mani](/wiki/Kalabhavan_Mani "Kalabhavan Mani"), [Indrans](/wiki/Indrans "Indrans"), [Shobana](/wiki/Shobana "Shobana"), [Nivin Pauly](/wiki/Nivin_Pauly "Nivin Pauly"), [Sreenivasan](/wiki/Sreenivasan "Sreenivasan"), [Urvashi](/wiki/Urvashi_(actress) "Urvashi (actress)"), [Manju Warrier](/wiki/Manju_Warrier "Manju Warrier"), [Suresh Gopi](/wiki/Suresh_Gopi "Suresh Gopi"), [Jayaram](/wiki/Jayaram "Jayaram"), [Murali](/wiki/Murali_(Malayalam_actor) "Murali (Malayalam actor)"), [Shankaradi](/wiki/Shankaradi "Shankaradi"), [Kavya Madhavan](/wiki/Kavya_Madhavan "Kavya Madhavan"), [Bhavana Menon](/wiki/Bhavana_Menon "Bhavana Menon"), [Prithviraj](/wiki/Prithviraj_Sukumaran "Prithviraj Sukumaran"), [Parvathy](/wiki/Parvathy_Thiruvothu "Parvathy Thiruvothu"), [Jayasurya](/wiki/Jayasurya "Jayasurya"), [Dulquer Salmaan](/wiki/Dulquer_Salmaan "Dulquer Salmaan"), [Oduvil Unnikrishnan](/wiki/Oduvil_Unnikrishnan "Oduvil Unnikrishnan"), [Jagathy Sreekumar](/wiki/Jagathy_Sreekumar "Jagathy Sreekumar"), [Nedumudi Venu](/wiki/Nedumudi_Venu "Nedumudi Venu"), [KPAC Lalitha](/wiki/KPAC_Lalitha "KPAC Lalitha"), [Innocent](/wiki/Innocent_(actor) "Innocent (actor)") and [Fahadh Faasil](/wiki/Fahadh_Faasil "Fahadh Faasil"). Late Malayalam actor Prem Nazir holds the world record for having acted as the protagonist of over 720 movies.[[464]](#cite_note-465) Since the 1980s, actors [Mohanlal](/wiki/Mohanlal "Mohanlal") and [Mammootty](/wiki/Mammootty "Mammootty") have dominated the movie industry; Mohanlal has won five National Film Awards (four for acting), while Mammootty has three National Film Awards for acting.[[465]](#cite_note-466) Malayalam Cinema has produced a few more notable personalities such as [K. J. Yesudas](/wiki/K._J._Yesudas "K. J. Yesudas"), [K.S. Chitra](/wiki/K.S._Chitra "K.S. Chitra"), [M.G. Sreekumar](/wiki/M._G._Sreekumar "M. G. Sreekumar"), [Vayalar Rama Varma](/wiki/Vayalar_Rama_Varma "Vayalar Rama Varma"), [V. Madhusoodanan Nair](/wiki/V._Madhusoodanan_Nair "V. Madhusoodanan Nair"), [M.T. Vasudevan Nair](/wiki/M.T._Vasudevan_Nair "M.T. Vasudevan Nair") and [O.N.V. Kurup](/wiki/O.N.V._Kurup "O.N.V. Kurup"),[[466]](#cite_note-indiatimes2-467) the last two mentioned being recipients of [Jnanpith award](/wiki/Jnanpith_award "Jnanpith award"), the highest literary award in India.[[467]](#cite_note-upscguide-468) [Resul Pookutty](/wiki/Resul_Pookutty "Resul Pookutty"), who is from Kerala, is the only Indian to win an [Academy Award for Best Sound Mixing](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Sound "Academy Award for Best Sound"), for the breakthrough film *[Slumdog Millionaire](/wiki/Slumdog_Millionaire "Slumdog Millionaire")*. As of 2018, [Malayalam cinema](/wiki/Malayalam_cinema "Malayalam cinema") has got 14 awards for the best actor, 6 for the best actress, 11 for the best film, and 13 for the best film director in the [National Film Awards](/wiki/National_Film_Awards "National Film Awards"), [India](/wiki/India "India").[[468]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018358-361-469)

### Literature

Main article: [Malayalam Literature](/wiki/Malayalam_Literature "Malayalam Literature")

The [Sangam literature](/wiki/Sangam_literature "Sangam literature") can be considered as the ancient predecessor of [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam").[[469]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018450-470) [Malayalam literature](/wiki/Malayalam_literature "Malayalam literature") starts from the [Old Malayalam](/wiki/Old_Malayalam "Old Malayalam") period (9th–13th century CE) and includes such notable writers as the 14th-century [Niranam poets](/wiki/Niranam_poets "Niranam poets") (Madhava Panikkar, Sankara Panikkar and Rama Panikkar),[[470]](#cite_note-Nair1967-471)[[471]](#cite_note-Laet1994-472) and the 16th-century poet [Thunchaththu Ezhuthachan](/wiki/Ezhuthachan "Ezhuthachan"), whose works mark the dawn of both the modern Malayalam language and its poetry.[[472]](#cite_note-George1998-473) For the first 600 years of [Malayalam calendar](/wiki/Kollam_Era "Kollam Era"), the literature mainly consisted of the oral [Ballads](/wiki/Ballad "Ballad") such as *[Vadakkan Pattukal](/wiki/Vadakkan_Pattukal "Vadakkan Pattukal")* in [North Malabar](/wiki/North_Malabar "North Malabar") and *Thekkan Pattukal* in [Southern Travancore](/wiki/Southern_Division_(Travancore) "Southern Division (Travancore)").[[473]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChandran2018453-474) Designated a "[Classical Language in India](/wiki/Classical_Language_in_India "Classical Language in India")" in 2013,[[362]](#cite_note-thehindu20130524-363) it developed into the current form mainly by the influence of the poets [Cherusseri Namboothiri](/wiki/Cherusseri_Namboothiri "Cherusseri Namboothiri"),[[474]](#cite_note-Cherussery_(Krishnagadha)_Malayalam_author_books-475)[[475]](#cite_note-mlm-476) [Thunchaththu Ezhuthachan](/wiki/Thunchaththu_Ezhuthachan "Thunchaththu Ezhuthachan"),[[475]](#cite_note-mlm-476) and [Poonthanam Nambudiri](/wiki/Poonthanam_Nambudiri "Poonthanam Nambudiri"),[[475]](#cite_note-mlm-476)[[476]](#cite_note-477) in the 15th and the 16th centuries of [Common Era](/wiki/Common_Era "Common Era").[[475]](#cite_note-mlm-476)[[477]](#cite_note-478) [Unnayi Variyar](/wiki/Unnayi_Variyar "Unnayi Variyar"),[[478]](#cite_note-Attakatha_sahithyam-479) a probable poet of the 17th/18th century CE, and [Kunchan Nambiar](/wiki/Kunchan_Nambiar "Kunchan Nambiar"), a poet of the 18th century CE, have also influenced a lot in the growth of modern Malayalam literature in its pre-mature form.[[475]](#cite_note-mlm-476) The [Bharathappuzha](/wiki/Bharathappuzha "Bharathappuzha") river, also known as [River Ponnani](/wiki/Ponnani "Ponnani"), and its tributaries, have played a major role in the development of modern Malayalam Literature.[[479]](#cite_note-480)

[Paremmakkal Thoma Kathanar](/wiki/Paremmakkal_Thoma_Kathanar "Paremmakkal Thoma Kathanar") and [Kerala Varma Valiakoi Thampuran](/wiki/Kerala_Varma_Valiakoi_Thampuran "Kerala Varma Valiakoi Thampuran") are noted for their contribution to Malayalam prose.[[480]](#cite_note-google24-481)[[481]](#cite_note-Sinhā2009-482)[[482]](#cite_note-Vilanilam1987-483) The "[triumvirate of poets](/wiki/Triumvirate_poets_of_modern_malayalam "Triumvirate poets of modern malayalam")" (*Kavithrayam*): [Kumaran Asan](/wiki/Kumaran_Asan "Kumaran Asan"), [Vallathol Narayana Menon](/wiki/Vallathol_Narayana_Menon "Vallathol Narayana Menon"), and [Ulloor S. Parameswara Iyer](/wiki/Ulloor_S._Parameswara_Iyer "Ulloor S. Parameswara Iyer"), are recognised for moving Keralite poetry away from archaic sophistry and metaphysics, and towards a more [lyrical](/wiki/Lyrics "Lyrics") mode.[[483]](#cite_note-Al̲ikkōṭȧ1979-484)[[484]](#cite_note-Indian_and_Foreign_Review-485)[[485]](#cite_note-Tarakan1990-486) The poets like [Moyinkutty Vaidyar](/wiki/Moyinkutty_Vaidyar "Moyinkutty Vaidyar") and [Pulikkottil Hyder](/wiki/Pulikkottil_Hyder "Pulikkottil Hyder") have made notable contributions to the [Mappila songs](/wiki/Mappila_songs "Mappila songs"), which is a genre of the [Arabi Malayalam](/wiki/Arabi_Malayalam "Arabi Malayalam") literature.[[486]](#cite_note-487)[[487]](#cite_note-488) The first travelogue in any Indian language is the Malayalam *[Varthamanappusthakam](/wiki/Varthamanappusthakam "Varthamanappusthakam")*, written by [Paremmakkal Thoma Kathanar](/wiki/Paremmakkal_Thoma_Kathanar "Paremmakkal Thoma Kathanar") in 1785.[[488]](#cite_note-legacy-489)[[489]](#cite_note-490) The prose literature, [Malayalam journalism](/wiki/Malayalam_journalism "Malayalam journalism"), and criticism began after the latter-half of the 18th century.[[488]](#cite_note-legacy-489) Contemporary Malayalam literature deals with social, political, and economic life context. The tendency of the modern literature is often towards [political radicalism](/wiki/Political_radicalism "Political radicalism").[[490]](#cite_note-491) [Malayalam literature](/wiki/Malayalam_literature "Malayalam literature") has been presented with 6 [Jnanapith awards](/wiki/Jnanpith_Award "Jnanpith Award"), the second-most for any Dravidian language and the third-highest for any Indian language.[[491]](#cite_note-492)[[492]](#cite_note-493) In the second half of the 20th century, [Jnanpith](/wiki/Jnanpith "Jnanpith") winning poets and writers like [G. Sankara Kurup](/wiki/G._Sankara_Kurup "G. Sankara Kurup"), [S. K. Pottekkatt](/wiki/S._K._Pottekkatt "S. K. Pottekkatt"), [Thakazhi Sivasankara Pillai](/wiki/Thakazhi_Sivasankara_Pillai "Thakazhi Sivasankara Pillai"), [M. T. Vasudevan Nair](/wiki/M._T._Vasudevan_Nair "M. T. Vasudevan Nair"), [O. N. V. Kurup](/wiki/O._N._V._Kurup "O. N. V. Kurup"), and [Akkitham Achuthan Namboothiri](/wiki/Akkitham_Achuthan_Namboothiri "Akkitham Achuthan Namboothiri"), had made valuable contributions to the modern Malayalam literature.[[493]](#cite_note-google25-494)[[494]](#cite_note-Accessions_List,_South_Asia-495)[[495]](#cite_note-Indian_Writing_Today-496)[[496]](#cite_note-DattaAkademi1987-497)[[497]](#cite_note-Malayalam_Literary_Survey-498) Later, writers like [O. V. Vijayan](/wiki/O._V._Vijayan "O. V. Vijayan"), [Kamaladas](/wiki/Kamaladas "Kamaladas"), [M. Mukundan](/wiki/M._Mukundan "M. Mukundan"), [Arundhati Roy](/wiki/Arundhati_Roy "Arundhati Roy"), [Vaikom Muhammed Basheer](/wiki/Vaikom_Muhammed_Basheer "Vaikom Muhammed Basheer"), have gained international recognition.[[498]](#cite_note-MukundanPillai2004-499)[[499]](#cite_note-Maheshwari2002-500)[[500]](#cite_note-Chaudhuri2008-501)[[501]](#cite_note-502)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Cuisine of Kerala](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Kerala "Cuisine of Kerala")

Kerala cuisine includes a wide variety of vegetarian and non-vegetarian dishes prepared using fish, poultry, and meat. Culinary spices have been cultivated in Kerala for millennia and they are characteristic of its cuisine.[[502]](#cite_note-google26-503) Rice is a dominant staple that is eaten at all times of day.[[503]](#cite_note-Majumdar2010-504) A majority of the breakfast foods in Kerala are made out of rice, in one form or the other (*[idli](/wiki/Idli "Idli")*, *[dosa](/wiki/Dosa_(food) "Dosa (food)")*, *[puttu](/wiki/Puttu "Puttu")*, *[pathiri](/wiki/Pathiri "Pathiri")*, *[appam](/wiki/Appam "Appam")*, or *[idiyappam](/wiki/Idiyappam "Idiyappam")*), [tapioca](/wiki/Tapioca "Tapioca") preparations, or pulse-based [vada](/wiki/Vada_(food) "Vada (food)").[[504]](#cite_note-Muthachen1970-505) These may be accompanied by [chutney](/wiki/Chutney "Chutney"), *[kadala](/wiki/Chickpea "Chickpea")*, *[payasam](/wiki/Payasam "Payasam")*, *[payar](/wiki/Mung_bean "Mung bean") [pappadam](/wiki/Pappadam "Pappadam")*, *[appam](/wiki/Appam "Appam")*, chicken curry, beef fry, egg masala and fish curry.[[241]](#cite_note-Newton-242) [Porotta](/wiki/Porotta "Porotta") and [Biryani](/wiki/Biryani "Biryani") are also often found in restaurants in Kerala. Thalassery biryani is popular as an ethnic brand. Lunch dishes include [rice and curry](/wiki/Rice_and_curry "Rice and curry") along with *[rasam](/wiki/Rasam_(dish) "Rasam (dish)")*, *pulisherry* and *[sambar](/wiki/Sambar_(dish) "Sambar (dish)")*.[[505]](#cite_note-google27-506) *[Sadhya](/wiki/Sadhya "Sadhya")* is a vegetarian meal, which is served on a banana leaf and followed with a cup of payasam.[[506]](#cite_note-Kannampilly2003-507) Popular snacks include [banana chips](/wiki/Banana_chip "Banana chip"), yam crisps, [tapioca chips](/wiki/Tapioca_chip "Tapioca chip"), [Achappam](/wiki/Achappam "Achappam"), [Unni appam](/wiki/Unni_appam "Unni appam") and *kuzhalappam*.[[507]](#cite_note-Kerala_with_Lakshadweep-508)[[508]](#cite_note-Koilparampil1982-509)[[509]](#cite_note-(Swami.)2000-510) Seafood specialties include [karimeen](/wiki/Karimeen "Karimeen"), prawns, shrimp and other crustacean dishes.[[510]](#cite_note-511) [Thalassery Cuisine](/wiki/Thalassery_Cuisine "Thalassery Cuisine") is varied and is a blend of many influences.

## Media

Main article: [Media in Kerala](/wiki/Media_in_Kerala "Media in Kerala")

The media, telecommunications, broadcasting and cable services are regulated by the [Telecom Regulatory Authority of India](/wiki/Telecom_Regulatory_Authority_of_India "Telecom Regulatory Authority of India") (TRAI).[[511]](#cite_note-512) The National Family Health Survey – 4, conducted in 2015–16, ranked Kerala as the state with the [highest media exposure in India](/wiki/Indian_states_ranking_by_media_exposure "Indian states ranking by media exposure").[[512]](#cite_note-513) Dozens of newspapers are published in Kerala, in nine major languages,[[513]](#cite_note-rniindia-514) but principally Malayalam and English.[[514]](#cite_note-google28-515) Kerala has the [highest media exposure in India](/wiki/Indian_states_ranking_by_media_exposure "Indian states ranking by media exposure").[[515]](#cite_note-516)[[516]](#cite_note-517) The most widely circulated [Malayalam-language newspapers](/wiki/List_of_Malayalam_language_newspapers "List of Malayalam language newspapers") are *[Malayala Manorama](/wiki/Malayala_Manorama "Malayala Manorama"), [Mathrubhumi](/wiki/Mathrubhumi "Mathrubhumi"), [Deshabhimani](/wiki/Deshabhimani "Deshabhimani"), [Madhyamam](/wiki/Madhyamam "Madhyamam"), [Kerala Kaumudi](/wiki/Kerala_Kaumudi "Kerala Kaumudi"), [Mangalam](/wiki/Mangalam_Publications "Mangalam Publications"), [Chandrika](/wiki/Chandrika_(Malayalam_newspaper) "Chandrika (Malayalam newspaper)"), [Deepika](/wiki/Deepika_(newspaper) "Deepika (newspaper)"), [Janayugam](/wiki/Janayugam "Janayugam"), [Janmabhumi](/wiki/Janmabhumi "Janmabhumi"), [Siraj Daily](/wiki/Siraj_Daily "Siraj Daily")* and *[Suprabhaatham](/wiki/Suprabhaatham_Daily "Suprabhaatham Daily")*. [Major Malayalam periodicals](/wiki/List_of_Malayalam-language_periodicals "List of Malayalam-language periodicals") include *[Mathrubhumi Azhchappathippu](/wiki/Mathrubhumi_Azhchappathippu "Mathrubhumi Azhchappathippu"), [Vanitha](/wiki/Vanitha "Vanitha"), [India Today](/wiki/India_Today "India Today") Malayalam, [Madhyamam Weekly](/wiki/Madhyamam_Weekly "Madhyamam Weekly"), [Grihalakshmi](/wiki/Grihalakshmi_(magazine) "Grihalakshmi (magazine)"), [Dhanam](/wiki/Dhanam_(business_magazine) "Dhanam (business magazine)"), [Chithrabhumi](/wiki/Chithrabhumi "Chithrabhumi")* and *[Bhashaposhini](/wiki/Bhashaposhini "Bhashaposhini")*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Malayala_Manorama_New_Press_in_Kollam-3%2C_Apr_2016.jpg/250px-Malayala_Manorama_New_Press_in_Kollam-3%2C_Apr_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malayala_Manorama_New_Press_in_Kollam-3,_Apr_2016.jpg)

[Malayala Manorama](/wiki/Malayala_Manorama "Malayala Manorama") office in [Kottiyam](/wiki/Kottiyam "Kottiyam"), [Kollam](/wiki/Kollam "Kollam")

[DD Malayalam](/wiki/DD_Malayalam "DD Malayalam") is a state-owned television broadcaster. [Multiple-system operators](/wiki/List_of_multiple-system_operators "List of multiple-system operators") provide a mix of Malayalam, English, other Indian languages, and international channels. Some of the popular Malayalam television channels are [Asianet](/wiki/Asianet_(TV_channel) "Asianet (TV channel)"), [Asianet News](/wiki/Asianet_News "Asianet News"), [Asianet Plus](/wiki/Asianet_Plus "Asianet Plus"), [Asianet Movies](/wiki/Asianet_Movies "Asianet Movies"), [Surya TV](/wiki/Surya_TV "Surya TV"), [Surya Movies](/wiki/Surya_Movies "Surya Movies"), [Mazhavil Manorama](/wiki/Mazhavil_Manorama "Mazhavil Manorama"), [Manorama News](/wiki/Manorama_News "Manorama News"), [Kairali TV](/wiki/Kairali_TV "Kairali TV"), [Kairali News](/wiki/Kairali_TV "Kairali TV"), [Flowers](/wiki/Flowers_(TV_channel) "Flowers (TV channel)"), [Media One TV](/wiki/Media_One_TV "Media One TV"), [Mathrubhumi News](/wiki/Mathrubhumi_News "Mathrubhumi News"), [Kappa TV](/wiki/Kappa_TV "Kappa TV"), [Amrita TV](/wiki/Amrita_TV "Amrita TV"), [Reporter TV](/wiki/Reporter_TV "Reporter TV"), [Jaihind](/wiki/Jaihind "Jaihind"), [Janam TV](/wiki/Janam_TV "Janam TV"), [Jeevan TV](/wiki/Jeevan_TV "Jeevan TV"), [Kaumudy TV](/wiki/Kaumudy_TV "Kaumudy TV") and [Shalom TV](/wiki/Shalom_(TV_channel) "Shalom (TV channel)"). With the second-highest internet penetration rate in India,[[517]](#cite_note-518) [Digital media](/wiki/Digital_media "Digital media") including [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media") and [OTT services](/wiki/Over-the-top_media_services_in_India "Over-the-top media services in India") are a main source of information and entertainment in the state. A sizeable [people's science movement](/wiki/People%27s_science_movement "People's science movement") has taken root in the state, and activities like writer's cooperatives are becoming increasingly common.[[201]](#cite_note-Tharamangalam_2005-202)[[518]](#cite_note-Ranjith_2004-519) [BSNL](/wiki/BSNL "BSNL"), [Airtel](/wiki/Bharti_Airtel "Bharti Airtel"), [Vodafone Idea Limited](/wiki/Vodafone_Idea_Limited "Vodafone Idea Limited"), [Jio](/wiki/Jio "Jio") are the major cell phone service providers.[[519]](#cite_note-520) Broadband Internet services are widely available throughout the state; some of the major [Internet service providers](/wiki/Internet_service_provider "Internet service provider") are [BSNL](/wiki/BSNL "BSNL"), [Asianet Satellite Communications](/wiki/Asianet_Satellite_Communications "Asianet Satellite Communications"), [Airtel](/wiki/Bharti_Airtel "Bharti Airtel"), [Vodafone Idea Limited](/wiki/Vodafone_Idea_Limited "Vodafone Idea Limited"), [RailWire](/wiki/RailTel "RailTel"), and [Tata Communications](/wiki/Tata_Communications "Tata Communications").

## Sports

Main article: [Sports in Kerala](/wiki/Sports_in_Kerala "Sports in Kerala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Kerala_boatrace.jpg/250px-Kerala_boatrace.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kerala_boatrace.jpg)

The annual [snake boat race](/wiki/Vallam_Kali "Vallam Kali") is performed during [Onam](/wiki/Onam "Onam") on the [Pamba River](/wiki/Pamba_River "Pamba River")

By the 21st century, almost all of the native sports and games from Kerala had either disappeared or become just an art form performed during local festivals; including [Poorakkali](/wiki/Poorakkali "Poorakkali"), Padayani, Thalappandukali, Onathallu, Parichamuttukali, Velakali, and Kilithattukali.[[520]](#cite_note-PRD-sports-521) However, *[Kalaripayattu](/wiki/Kalaripayattu "Kalaripayattu")*, regarded as "the mother of all martial arts in the world", is an exception and is practised as the indigenous martial sport.[[521]](#cite_note-522) Another traditional sport of Kerala is the boat race, especially the race of [Snake boats](/wiki/Snake_boat "Snake boat").[[520]](#cite_note-PRD-sports-521)

[Cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket") and [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") became popular in the state; both were introduced in Malabar during the British colonial period in the 19th century. Cricketers, like [Tinu Yohannan](/wiki/Tinu_Yohannan "Tinu Yohannan"), [Abey Kuruvilla](/wiki/Abey_Kuruvilla "Abey Kuruvilla"), [Chundangapoyil Rizwan](/wiki/Chundangapoyil_Rizwan "Chundangapoyil Rizwan"), [Sreesanth](/wiki/Sreesanth "Sreesanth"), [Sanju Samson](/wiki/Sanju_Samson "Sanju Samson") and [Basil Thampi](/wiki/Basil_Thampi "Basil Thampi") found places in the national cricket team. A cricket franchise from Kerala, the [Kochi Tuskers](/wiki/Kochi_Tuskers_Kerala "Kochi Tuskers Kerala"), played in the [Indian Premier League](/wiki/Indian_Premier_League "Indian Premier League")'s [fourth season](/wiki/2011_Indian_Premier_League "2011 Indian Premier League"). However, this team was disbanded after the season because of conflicts of interest among its franchises.[[522]](#cite_note-India_Wins_World_Twenty20_Thriller-523)[[523]](#cite_note-hindu_jul09-524) Kerala has only performed well recently in the [Ranji Trophy](/wiki/Ranji_Trophy "Ranji Trophy") cricket competition, in 2017–18 reaching the quarterfinals for the first time in history.[[520]](#cite_note-PRD-sports-521)[[524]](#cite_note-525) Football is one of the most widely played and watched sports with huge in this state support for club and district level matches. Kochi hosts [Kerala Blasters FC](/wiki/Kerala_Blasters_FC "Kerala Blasters FC") in the [Indian Super League](/wiki/Indian_Super_League "Indian Super League"). The Blasters are one of the most widely supported clubs in the country as well as the fifth most-followed football club from [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia") in social media.[[525]](#cite_note-526)[[526]](#cite_note-527)[[527]](#cite_note-528) Also, Kozhikode hosts [Gokulam Kerala FC](/wiki/Gokulam_Kerala_FC "Gokulam Kerala FC") in the [I-League](/wiki/I-League "I-League") as well as the [Sait Nagjee Football Tournament](/wiki/Sait_Nagjee_Football_Tournament "Sait Nagjee Football Tournament"). Kerala is one of the major footballing states in India along with West Bengal and Goa and has produced national players like [I. M. Vijayan](/wiki/I._M._Vijayan "I. M. Vijayan"), C. V. Pappachan, [V. P. Sathyan](/wiki/V._P._Sathyan "V. P. Sathyan"), [U. Sharaf Ali](/wiki/U._Sharaf_Ali "U. Sharaf Ali"), [Jo Paul Ancheri](/wiki/Jo_Paul_Ancheri "Jo Paul Ancheri"), [Ashique Kuruniyan](/wiki/Ashique_Kuruniyan "Ashique Kuruniyan"), [Muhammad Rafi](/wiki/Mohammed_Rafi_(footballer) "Mohammed Rafi (footballer)"), [Jiju Jacob](/wiki/Jiju_Jacob "Jiju Jacob"), [Mashoor Shereef](/wiki/Mashoor_Shereef "Mashoor Shereef"), [Pappachen Pradeep](/wiki/Pappachen_Pradeep "Pappachen Pradeep"), [C.K. Vineeth](/wiki/C.K._Vineeth "C.K. Vineeth"), [Anas Edathodika](/wiki/Anas_Edathodika "Anas Edathodika"), [Sahal Abdul Samad](/wiki/Sahal_Abdul_Samad "Sahal Abdul Samad"), and [Rino Anto](/wiki/Rino_Anto "Rino Anto").[[528]](#cite_note-529)[[529]](#cite_note-530)[[530]](#cite_note-india-531)[[531]](#cite_note-532)[[532]](#cite_note-533) The Kerala state football team has won the [Santhosh Trophy](/wiki/Santhosh_Trophy "Santhosh Trophy") seven times; in 1973, 1992, 1993, 2001, 2004, 2018, and 2022. They were also the runners-up eight times.[[533]](#cite_note-534)

Among the prominent athletes hailing from the state are [P. T. Usha](/wiki/P._T._Usha "P. T. Usha"), [Shiny Wilson](/wiki/Shiny_Wilson "Shiny Wilson") and [M.D. Valsamma](/wiki/M.D._Valsamma "M.D. Valsamma"), all three of whom are recipients of the [Padma Shri](/wiki/Padma_Shri "Padma Shri") as well as [Arjuna Award](/wiki/Arjuna_Award "Arjuna Award"), while [K. Yohannan](/wiki/T._C._Yohannan "T. C. Yohannan"), [Suresh Babu](/wiki/Suresh_Babu_(long_jumper) "Suresh Babu (long jumper)"), [Sinimol Paulose](/wiki/Sinimol_Paulose "Sinimol Paulose"), Angel Mary Joseph, [Mercy Kuttan](/wiki/Mercy_Kuttan "Mercy Kuttan"), K. Saramma, [K. C. Rosakutty](/wiki/Rosa_Kutty "Rosa Kutty"), Padmini Selvan and Tintu Luka are the other Arjuna Award winners from Kerala.[[520]](#cite_note-PRD-sports-521)[[534]](#cite_note-535) Volleyball is another popular sport and is often played on makeshift courts on sandy beaches along the coast.[[535]](#cite_note-536) [Jimmy George](/wiki/Jimmy_George "Jimmy George") was a notable Indian volleyball player, rated in his prime as among the world's ten best players.[[536]](#cite_note-537) Other popular sports include [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton"), [basketball](/wiki/Basketball "Basketball") and [kabaddi](/wiki/Kabaddi "Kabaddi").[[537]](#cite_note-Reddy2005-538) The Indian Hockey team captain [P. R. Shreejesh](/wiki/P._R._Shreejesh "P. R. Shreejesh"), ace goalkeeper hails from Kerala. International Walkers from the state include [K. T. Irfan](/wiki/K._T._Irfan "K. T. Irfan").[[538]](#cite_note-539)

For the [2017 FIFA U-17 World Cup](/wiki/2017_FIFA_U-17_World_Cup "2017 FIFA U-17 World Cup") in [India](/wiki/India "India"), the [Jawaharlal Nehru Stadium (Kochi)](/wiki/Jawaharlal_Nehru_Stadium_(Kochi) "Jawaharlal Nehru Stadium (Kochi)"), was chosen as one of the six venues where the game would be hosted in [India](/wiki/India "India").[[539]](#cite_note-540) [Greenfield International Stadium](/wiki/Greenfield_International_Stadium "Greenfield International Stadium") at located at Kariavattom in Thiruvananthapuram city, is India's first DBOT (design, build, operate and transfer) model outdoor stadium and it has hosted international cricket matches and international football matches including [2015 SAFF Championship](/wiki/2015_SAFF_Championship "2015 SAFF Championship").[[540]](#cite_note-SAFF_Cup_Dates-541)

## Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Kerala](/wiki/Tourism_in_Kerala "Tourism in Kerala")

Kerala's culture and traditions, coupled with its varied [demographics](/wiki/Demographics_of_Kerala "Demographics of Kerala"), have made the state one of the most popular tourist destinations in India. In 2012, [National Geographic](/wiki/National_Geographic_Society "National Geographic Society")'s [Traveller](/wiki/National_Geographic_Traveler "National Geographic Traveler") magazine named Kerala as one of the "ten paradises of the world"[[541]](#cite_note-Kerala_Tourism-542)[[542]](#cite_note-543)[[543]](#cite_note-544) and "50 must see destinations of a lifetime".[[544]](#cite_note-Kerala_India-545) [Travel and Leisure](/wiki/Travel_and_Leisure "Travel and Leisure") also described Kerala as "One of the 100 great trips for the 21st century".[[541]](#cite_note-Kerala_Tourism-542)[[545]](#cite_note-546) In 2012, it overtook the [Taj Mahal](/wiki/Taj_Mahal "Taj Mahal") to be the number one travel destination in Google's search trends for India.[[546]](#cite_note-547) [CNN Travel](/wiki/CNN_Travel "CNN Travel") listed Kerala among its '19 best places to visit in 2019'.[[547]](#cite_note-548) Kerala was named by [TIME magazine](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)") in 2022 among the 50 extraordinary destinations to explore in its list of the World's Greatest Places.[[548]](#cite_note-549) In 2025, Kerala was ranked 16th in the [Rough Guides](/wiki/Rough_Guides "Rough Guides")' list of the “World’s Top 26 Destinations for 2026”, making it the only Indian state to be included in the international travel guide’s annual global destinations ranking.[[549]](#cite_note-550)

Kerala's beaches, backwaters, lakes, mountain ranges, waterfalls, ancient ports, palaces, religious institutions[[550]](#cite_note-Ltd.2012-551) and wildlife sanctuaries are major attractions for both domestic and international tourists.[[551]](#cite_note-Tripays-552) The city of Kochi ranks first in the total number of international and domestic tourists in Kerala.[[552]](#cite_note-keralatourism-553)[[553]](#cite_note-Tourism_Statistics-554) Until the early 1980s, Kerala was a relatively unknown destination compared to other states in the country.[[554]](#cite_note-HinduTourism-555) In 1986 the government of Kerala declared tourism an important industry and it was the first state in India to do so.[[541]](#cite_note-Kerala_Tourism-542) Marketing campaigns launched by the [Kerala Tourism Development Corporation](/wiki/Kerala_Tourism_Development_Corporation "Kerala Tourism Development Corporation"), the government agency that oversees the tourism prospects of the state, resulted in the growth of the tourism industry.[[555]](#cite_note-Tourism-556) Many advertisements branded Kerala with the tagline *[Kerala, God's Own Country](/wiki/God%27s_Own_Country "God's Own Country")*.[[555]](#cite_note-Tourism-556) Kerala tourism is a global brand and regarded as one of the destinations with highest recall.[[555]](#cite_note-Tourism-556) In 2006, Kerala attracted 8.5 million tourists, an increase of 23.7% over the previous year, making the state one of the fastest-growing popular destinations in the world.[[556]](#cite_note-557) In 2011, tourist inflow to Kerala crossed the 10-million mark.[[557]](#cite_note-bizstd-558)

Ayurvedic tourism has become very popular since the 1990s, and private agencies have played a notable role in tandem with the initiatives of the Tourism Department.[[554]](#cite_note-HinduTourism-555) Kerala is known for its [ecotourism](/wiki/Ecotourism "Ecotourism") initiatives which include mountaineering, trekking and bird-watching programmes in the Western Ghats as the major activities.[[558]](#cite_note-Devrep47-559) The state's tourism industry is a major contributor to the [state's economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Kerala "Economy of Kerala"), growing at the rate of 13.3%.[[559]](#cite_note-560) The revenue from tourism increased five-fold between 2001 and 2011 and crossed the ₹ 190 billion mark in 2011. According to the Economic Times[[560]](#cite_note-561) Kerala netted a record revenue of INR 365280.1 million from the tourism sector in 2018, clocking an increase of Rs 28743.3 million from the previous year. Over 16.7 million tourists visited Kerala in 2018 as against 15.76 million the previous year, recording an increase of 5.9%. The industry provides employment to approximately 1.2 million people.[[557]](#cite_note-bizstd-558)

The state's only drive-in beach, [Muzhappilangad Beach](/wiki/Muzhappilangad_Beach "Muzhappilangad Beach") in Kannur, which stretches across 5 kilometres (3.1 mi) of sand, was chosen by the BBC as one of the top six drive-in beaches in the world in 2016.[[561]](#cite_note-562) [Idukki Dam](/wiki/Idukki_Dam "Idukki Dam"), the world's second arch dam, and Asia's first is at [Idukki](/wiki/Idukki "Idukki"). The major beaches are at [Kovalam](/wiki/Kovalam "Kovalam"), [Varkala](/wiki/Varkala "Varkala"), [Kozhikode](/wiki/Kozhikode "Kozhikode"), [Fort Kochi](/wiki/Fort_Kochi "Fort Kochi"), [Cherai](/wiki/Cherai "Cherai"), [Alappuzha](/wiki/Alappuzha "Alappuzha"), [Ponnani](/wiki/Ponnani "Ponnani"), [Kadalundi](/wiki/Kadalundi "Kadalundi"), [Tanur](/wiki/Tanur,_Malappuram "Tanur, Malappuram"), [Chaliyam](/wiki/Chaliyam "Chaliyam"), [Payyambalam](/wiki/Payyambalam "Payyambalam"), Kappad, [Muzhappilangad](/wiki/Muzhappilangad_Beach "Muzhappilangad Beach") and [Bekal](/wiki/Bekal "Bekal"). Popular [hill stations](/wiki/Hill_station "Hill station") are at [Ponmudi](/wiki/Ponmudi "Ponmudi"), [Wayanad](/wiki/Wayanad "Wayanad"), [Vagamon](/wiki/Vagamon "Vagamon"), [Munnar](/wiki/Munnar "Munnar"), [Peermade](/wiki/Peermade "Peermade"), [Ramakkalmedu](/wiki/Ramakkalmedu "Ramakkalmedu"), [Arimbra](/wiki/Arimbra_Hills,_Malappuram "Arimbra Hills, Malappuram"), [Paithalmala](/wiki/Paithalmala "Paithalmala") of [Kannur](/wiki/Idukki "Idukki") district, [Kodikuthimala](/wiki/Kodikuthimala "Kodikuthimala"), and [Nelliampathi](/wiki/Nelliampathi "Nelliampathi").[[562]](#cite_note-563) Munnar is 4,500 feet above sea level and is known for tea plantations, and a variety of flora and fauna.[[563]](#cite_note-FPJ-564) Kerala's ecotourism destinations include 12 wildlife sanctuaries and two national parks: [Periyar Tiger Reserve](/wiki/Periyar_Tiger_Reserve "Periyar Tiger Reserve"), [Parambikulam Wildlife Sanctuary](/wiki/Parambikulam_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Parambikulam Wildlife Sanctuary"), [Chinnar Wildlife Sanctuary](/wiki/Chinnar_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Chinnar Wildlife Sanctuary"), [Thattekad Bird Sanctuary](/wiki/Thattekad_Bird_Sanctuary "Thattekad Bird Sanctuary"), [Wayanad Wildlife Sanctuary](/wiki/Wayanad_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Wayanad Wildlife Sanctuary"), [Kadalundi Bird Sanctuary](/wiki/Kadalundi_Bird_Sanctuary "Kadalundi Bird Sanctuary"), [Karimpuzha Wildlife Sanctuary](/wiki/Karimpuzha_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Karimpuzha Wildlife Sanctuary"), [Muthanga](/wiki/Muthanga "Muthanga") Wildlife Sanctuary, [Aralam Wildlife Sanctuary](/wiki/Aralam_Wildlife_Sanctuary "Aralam Wildlife Sanctuary"), [Eravikulam National Park](/wiki/Eravikulam_National_Park "Eravikulam National Park"), and [Silent Valley National Park](/wiki/Silent_Valley_National_Park "Silent Valley National Park") are the most popular among them.[[564]](#cite_note-565) The Kerala backwaters are an extensive network of interlocking rivers (41 west-flowing rivers), lakes, and canals that centre around Alleppey, [Kumarakom](/wiki/Kumarakom "Kumarakom"), [Ponnani](/wiki/Ponnani "Ponnani"), [Nileshwaram](/wiki/Nileshwaram "Nileshwaram"), and [Punnamada](/wiki/Punnamada "Punnamada") (where the annual Nehru Trophy Boat Race is held in August), [Pathiramanal](/wiki/Pathiramanal "Pathiramanal") a small island in [Muhamma](/wiki/Muhamma "Muhamma"). [Padmanabhapuram Palace](/wiki/Padmanabhapuram_Palace "Padmanabhapuram Palace") and the [Mattancherry Palace](/wiki/Mattancherry_Palace "Mattancherry Palace") are two nearby heritage sites.[[565]](#cite_note-566)[[566]](#cite_note-567)

408. **[^](#cite_ref-409)** [Pingree, David](/wiki/David_Pingree "David Pingree") (1992), "Hellenophilia versus the History of Science", *Isis*, **83** (4): 554–63, [Bibcode](/wiki/Bibcode_(identifier) "Bibcode (identifier)"):[1992Isis...83..554P](https://ui.adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/1992Isis...83..554P), [doi](/wiki/Doi_(identifier) "Doi (identifier)"):[10.1086/356288](https://doi.org/10.1086%2F356288), [JSTOR](/wiki/JSTOR_(identifier) "JSTOR (identifier)") [234257](https://www.jstor.org/stable/234257), [S2CID](/wiki/S2CID_(identifier) "S2CID (identifier)") [68570164](https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusID:68570164), "One example I can give you relates to the Indian Mādhava's demonstration, in about 1400 A.D., of the infinite power series of trigonometrical functions using geometrical and algebraic arguments. When this was first described in English by Charles Whish, in the 1830s, it was heralded as the Indians' discovery of the calculus. This claim and Mādhava's achievements were ignored by Western historians, presumably at first because they could not admit that an Indian discovered the calculus, but later because no one read anymore the *Transactions of the Royal Asiatic Society*, in which Whish's article was published. The matter resurfaced in the 1950s, and now we have the Sanskrit texts properly edited, and we understand the clever way that Mādhava derived the series *without* the calculus, but many historians still find it impossible to conceive of the problem and its solution in terms of anything other than the calculus and proclaim that the calculus is what Mādhava found. In this case, the elegance and brilliance of Mādhava's mathematics are being distorted as they are buried under the current mathematical solution to a problem to which he discovered an alternate and powerful solution."
409. **[^](#cite_ref-410)** ["Missionaries led State to renaissance: Pinarayi"](https://www.thehindu.com/news/national/kerala/Missionaries-led-State-to-renaissance-Pinarayi/article16444369.ece). *The Hindu*. 13 November 2016. "Inaugurating on Saturday the valedictory of the bicentenary celebration of the arrival of Church Mission Society (CMS) missionaries to the shores of Kerala, Mr. Vijayan said it was their pioneering work in the fields of education, literature, printing, publishing, women's education, education of the differently-abled and, in general, a new social approach through the inclusion of marginalised sections into the mainstream which brought the idea of 'equality' into the realm of public consciousness. This had raised the standard of public consciousness and paved the way for the emergence of the renaissance movements in the State."
